nodes.label: Claude Code offers four ways to parallelize work — subagents, agent view, agent teams, and dynamic workflows — each differing in who coordinates, whether workers communicate, and whether they isolate files; the right choice depends on staying in-conversation vs handing off vs having Claude coordinate a group.

Subagents — delegated workers inside ONE session that do a side task in their OWN context and return only a summary to the spawning conversation; use when a side task would flood the main conversation with search results, logs, or file contents you won't reference again.

Agent view (`claude agents`, research preview) — one screen to dispatch and MONITOR sessions running in the background, showing each session's state and which need input; use for several independent hand-off tasks you check at a glance and step into only when needed. Sessions report only to you.

Agent teams — multiple coordinated sessions with a SHARED task list and inter-agent messaging, managed by a lead; EXPERIMENTAL and disabled by default. Use when you want Claude to split a project, assign pieces, and keep workers in sync. Teammates are NOT worktree-isolated.

Dynamic workflows — a SCRIPT (not Claude's turn-by-turn judgment) that runs many subagents and cross-checks their results; for work too big for one turn or needing more than a single pass: codebase-wide audits, 500-file migrations, cross-checked research, multi-angle plans.
In every parallelization approach the workers are Claude sessions; to involve a DIFFERENT tool you must expose it to Claude as an MCP server.
To bring a non-Claude tool into any agent approach, expose it to Claude as an MCP server (/docs/en/mcp) — the only integration path for external tools.

Worktrees give each session a SEPARATE git checkout so parallel sessions never edit the same files; for self-run sessions. Agent view auto-moves each dispatched session into its own worktree, and spawned subagents can each get one too.

`/batch` — a skill that has Claude split one large change into 5-to-30 worktree-isolated subagents that EACH open a pull request; a packaged use of subagents + worktrees, not a separate coordination style.
A background bash command runs one shell command WITHOUT blocking the conversation and does NOT spawn an agent — distinct from agent parallelization.
A forked subagent, started with `/subtask`, is a subagent that INHERITS your full conversation context instead of starting fresh — a way to spawn a subagent, not a separate surface.

`/fork` copies the whole session into a new background session that runs ALONGSIDE it; and with agent view turned OFF, `/fork` (not `/subtask`) becomes the forked-subagent command and `/subtask` is unavailable.
A routine runs a session on a SCHEDULE in Anthropic's cloud — not in parallel on your machine; solves scheduling, not work-splitting.
Running several sessions or subagents at once MULTIPLIES token usage; consult Costs for usage and rate-limit details — the core cost constraint on parallelization.

Choose an approach by three questions: WHO coordinates (Claude in one conversation → subagents; you hand off → agent view; Claude plans/assigns/supervises → agent teams; a script holds the plan → dynamic workflows), whether workers must COMMUNICATE, and whether tasks touch the SAME files.

Communication differs by approach: subagents report results back to the spawning conversation, agent view sessions report only to you, and agent-team teammates share a task list and message each other DIRECTLY.

Tasks touching the same files must be isolated with worktrees; subagents and self-run sessions can each use a separate worktree, but agent teams do NOT isolate teammates — you must partition the work so each teammate owns a different file set.

Checking on running work depends on approach: `claude agents` (agent view) for background sessions, @-mention typeahead for named background subagents, `/tasks` for anything in the current session's background (incl. finished subagents), `/workflows` for dynamic-workflow runs.
`/tasks` lists each item running in the current session's background and lets you check on, attach to, or stop it; the list also includes subagents that have finished.
`/workflows` lists running and completed dynamic-workflow runs, the phase each is in, and how many agents have finished.

As of v2.1.198, `/agents` no longer opens a panel — it prints a notice pointing to subagent file locations; create/edit custom subagents by asking Claude or editing files directly. `/agents` is SEPARATE from `claude agents` despite the similar name.
Dynamic workflows can VERIFY subagent findings against each other (cross-checked research, multi-angle plans) — the distinguishing capability that makes them fit jobs needing more than a single pass.

Track cost and usage in the Claude Agent SDK: how to read per-step token usage, per-model cost, cumulative total_cost_usd, deduplicate parallel tool calls, account for subagents, handle errors, and configure prompt caching TTL.

total_cost_usd/costUSD are CLIENT-SIDE ESTIMATES from a price table bundled at SDK build time — they drift on pricing changes, unrecognized models, or unmodeled billing rules; never bill end users or trigger financial decisions from them.
For AUTHORITATIVE billing (not the SDK's local estimate) use the Usage and Cost API or the Usage page in the Claude Console.

Cost data is scoped at three levels: query() call (one SDK invocation, produces one result message), step (one request/response cycle within a call, produces assistant messages), and session (query() calls linked by session ID via the resume option; each call reports cost independently).
A single query() call runs an agent loop of multiple steps (Claude responds, uses tools, gets results, responds again) and emits exactly one result message marking the loop's end.

TypeScript and Python SDKs expose the same cost model/granularity but differ in field names: TS uses message.message.usage, modelUsage, camelCase; Python uses message.usage, message.message_id, model_usage, usage dict.

The result message (SDKResultMessage / ResultMessage) marks the end of a query() call's agent loop and carries total_cost_usd, cumulative usage, and modelUsage/model_usage; emitted for both success and error results.
total_cost_usd on the result message = cumulative estimated cost across all steps of that single query() call (INCLUDING subagent requests); each call's result reflects only that call's cost.

When an agent spawns subagents, result fields differ: `usage` EXCLUDES subagent tokens (top-level loop only, undercounts on nesting); `total_cost_usd` and `modelUsage`/`model_usage` INCLUDE subagent requests — use modelUsage for whole-tree accounting.

modelUsage/model_usage on the result message maps model name → per-model token counts and cost (costUSD, inputTokens, outputTokens, cacheReadInputTokens, cacheCreationInputTokens); the correct field for whole-tree, per-model token accounting across subagents.
Running different models per role (e.g. Haiku for subagents, Opus for the main agent) is a supported orchestration pattern; modelUsage reveals where tokens/cost go per model.
Per-step token tracking: accumulate input_tokens/output_tokens from each assistant message's nested BetaMessage.usage, tracking seen message.id in a Set and counting each unique ID only once.
Parallel tool calls in one turn produce MULTIPLE assistant messages that share the same nested BetaMessage id with identical usage — always deduplicate by id or per-step token totals are inflated.
The SDK provides NO session-level total; each query() returns its own total_cost_usd, so applications making multiple calls must accumulate the totals themselves.
Pattern for cross-call cost: loop prompts, run query() per prompt, add each result's total_cost_usd to a running total; wrap each in try/catch so a failed call continues to the next prompt.

A single-shot query() throws/raises AFTER yielding an error result; both success and error result messages carry usage and total_cost_usd, so always read cost from the result regardless of subtype — connection/process failures yield no result message at all.

Rarely, messages sharing an id report different output_tokens — use the highest value (final message in a group is accurate), prefer the result message's total_cost_usd over summing per-step yourself, and report inconsistencies to the claude-code GitHub repo.

The Agent SDK AUTOMATICALLY uses prompt caching to cut costs on repeated content — no configuration needed; usage exposes cache_creation_input_tokens (higher rate) and cache_read_input_tokens (reduced rate).

Cache token fields: cache_creation_input_tokens (creating new cache entries, charged higher than standard input) and cache_read_input_tokens (reads from existing cache, reduced rate); track separately from input_tokens to see caching savings.

Cache TTL defaults to 5 minutes (API key / Bedrock / Google Agent Platform / Microsoft Foundry); set ENABLE_PROMPT_CACHING_1H=1 (shell or options.env) for a 1-hour TTL — beneficial for many short sessions with >5min gaps; 1h writes cost more but yield more reads. Claude subscription users get 1h automatically.

Claude Agent SDK overview — build production AI agents with Claude Code as a library (Python/TypeScript), providing the same tools, agent loop, and context management that power Claude Code, with built-in tool execution, subagents, hooks, MCP, permissions, and sessions.

query() — the core Agent SDK entrypoint: takes a prompt + ClaudeAgentOptions and returns an async iterator of messages while Claude autonomously runs the agent loop (reads files, runs commands, edits code).
The Agent SDK runs the full agent loop autonomously — Claude handles tool execution itself, unlike the Client SDK where you implement the tool-use while-loop manually.
For languages other than Python/TypeScript, run the CLI programmatically (headless) with the `-p` flag and `--output-format json` to drive the same agent.

allowed_tools / allowedTools — ClaudeAgentOptions field pre-approving tools (e.g. Read, Edit, Bash) so they run without a permission prompt; unlisted tools still run but fall through to the permission mode.
disallowed_tools — ClaudeAgentOptions field to block tools entirely (harder than allowed_tools which only pre-approves).
permission_mode / permissionMode — controls default handling of tools not in allowed_tools; e.g. `acceptEdits` auto-accepts file edits without prompting.
Built-in tools ship with the SDK: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Monitor, Glob, Grep, WebSearch, WebFetch, AskUserQuestion — plus scheduling and worktree tools in the full tools-reference.
Monitor tool — watches a background script and reacts to each output line as an event, enabling long-running / background-process observation.
AskUserQuestion tool — asks the user clarifying questions with multiple-choice options; used for interactive approval / human-in-the-loop input.
Subagents — spawn specialized agents to handle focused subtasks; the main agent delegates work and subagents report results back, the core multi-agent delegation primitive.

AgentDefinition — defines a custom subagent with a description, a specialized prompt, and its own restricted tools list (e.g. code-reviewer with Read/Glob/Grep), passed via the `agents` option keyed by name.
Agent tool — subagents are invoked through the Agent tool, so `Agent` must be in allowedTools to auto-approve subagent invocations.
parent_tool_use_id — field on messages emitted from within a subagent's context, letting an orchestrator track which messages belong to which subagent execution.

Subagents run focused subtasks with their own restricted tool sets so the main agent can delegate and stay context-lean — specialization + isolation is why to use subagents rather than one monolithic agent.

SDK hooks — callback functions run at key agent-lifecycle points to validate, log, block, or transform behavior; available hooks include PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, SessionStart, SessionEnd, UserPromptSubmit, and more.
HookMatcher — binds a hook callback to matching tool events via a matcher regex (e.g. matcher="Edit|Write" for PostToolUse), the mechanism for scoping which tools trigger a hook.

MCP support — connect the agent to external systems (databases, browsers, APIs) via Model Context Protocol servers declared in mcp_servers; tools are exposed as `mcp__<server>__*` and enabled via allowed_tools.
Sessions — maintain context across multiple query() exchanges; Claude remembers files read, analysis done, and conversation history, and sessions can be resumed or forked.
resume — ClaudeAgentOptions field taking a captured session_id to continue a prior query with full context; session_id is read from the SystemMessage with subtype=="init".
Sessions can be FORKED to explore different approaches from the same accumulated context — a branch primitive for an orchestrator running parallel strategies.
Agent SDK session state is stored as JSONL on your own filesystem (vs Managed Agents' Anthropic-hosted event log).
ResultMessage / messages with a `result` field — the terminal message carrying the agent's final output; a single-shot query() raises/throws after yielding an error result.

The SDK loads Claude Code filesystem config (skills, commands, memory/CLAUDE.md, plugins) from `.claude/` in the working dir and `~/.claude/`; restrict which sources load via setting_sources / settingSources.
Skills — specialized capabilities Claude uses automatically or invokes with `/name`, loaded from `.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md`; preferred over legacy commands for new custom commands.
Plugins — extend the agent with skills, agents, hooks, and MCP servers, configured programmatically via the `plugins` option.

Authentication: ANTHROPIC_API_KEY by default, plus third-party providers via env flags — CLAUDE_CODE_USE_BEDROCK, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_ANTHROPIC_AWS (+ANTHROPIC_AWS_WORKSPACE_ID), CLAUDE_CODE_USE_VERTEX, CLAUDE_CODE_USE_FOUNDRY.
Both the TypeScript and Python SDKs bundle a native Claude Code binary per platform, so Claude Code need not be installed separately; Python SDK requires Python 3.10+.
Agent SDK vs Client SDK: the Client SDK gives raw API access where you implement the tool loop yourself; the Agent SDK gives Claude with built-in tool execution handling the loop for you.

Agent SDK vs Managed Agents: Agent SDK runs the loop in your own process/infrastructure on your files; Managed Agents is a hosted REST API where Anthropic runs the agent in a managed per-session sandbox — best for long-running/async production without operating sandbox infra.
Agent SDK vs Claude Code CLI (same capabilities): CLI for interactive dev / one-off tasks; SDK for CI/CD, custom applications, and production automation — workflows translate directly between them.
Permissions let you control exactly which tools an agent can use — allow safe ops, block dangerous ones, require approval for sensitive actions — the safety boundary for autonomous execution.

Agent Skills in the Claude Agent SDK: how SKILL.md filesystem artifacts are discovered, filtered via the `skills` option, model-invoked, and how tool access is controlled — distinct from programmatically-defined subagents.
An Agent Skill is a specialized capability packaged as a SKILL.md file (instructions + description + optional supporting resources) that Claude autonomously invokes when relevant.
Skills MUST be created as filesystem artifacts (SKILL.md); unlike subagents, the SDK provides NO programmatic API for registering Skills.

Subagents can be defined programmatically (with programmatic options) while Skills are filesystem-only; Subagents-in-the-SDK are the 'similar filesystem-based agents' with a programmatic path for orchestration.
Skills are model-invoked: Claude autonomously chooses when to use a Skill based on context, matching its description — not explicitly called by the developer.
Skill metadata is discovered at startup from user and project directories; full Skill content is loaded only when triggered — a progressive-disclosure pattern that limits context cost.
Loading only metadata at startup and full content on trigger keeps the model's context lean until a Skill is actually needed — the token/cost-control rationale behind lazy Skill loading.
The `skills` option on query(): omit = discovered Skills enabled + Skill tool available (CLI parity); `"all"` = enable all; a name list = enable only those; `[]` = disable all.
Skill names in the `skills` list match the `name` field in SKILL.md or the Skill's directory name; use `plugin:skill` syntax for plugin-provided Skills.
The `skills` option is a CONTEXT FILTER, not a sandbox: unlisted Skills are hidden from the model and rejected by the Skill tool, but their files stay on disk and remain reachable via Read and Bash.
Filtering Skills reduces what the model sees (context) but does not restrict filesystem access — so tool access must be constrained separately, not assumed from the skills filter.
Skills load only when `settingSources` (TS) / `setting_sources` (Py) includes `'user'` or `'project'`; setting sources explicitly to `[]` loads NO skills — the #1 'Skills Not Found' cause.
With default query() options the SDK loads user and project sources, so skills in `~/.claude/skills/`, `<cwd>/.claude/skills/`, and `.claude/skills/` in any parent up to the repo root are available.
Setting the `skills` option auto-adds the `Skill` tool to `allowedTools`; but if you also pass an explicit `tools` list you must include `"Skill"` yourself or Claude cannot invoke skills.
Project Skills live in `.claude/skills/`, are shared with a team via git, and load when setting_sources includes `"project"`.
User Skills live in `~/.claude/skills/`, are personal across all projects, and load when setting_sources includes `"user"`.
Plugin Skills are bundled with installed Claude Code plugins; the `plugins` option can load skills from a specific path instead of the default setting sources.

The SDK loads Skills from `.claude/skills/` in the `cwd` option and every parent directory up to the repo root; `cwd` must point at or below the directory containing `.claude/skills/`, within the same repo.
A Skill is a directory containing a SKILL.md file with YAML frontmatter + Markdown content; the `description` field determines when Claude invokes the Skill.
The SKILL.md `description` is the trigger signal: it must be specific and include relevant keywords, or Claude won't auto-invoke the Skill (a 'Skill Not Being Used' cause).
The `allowed-tools` frontmatter field in SKILL.md is honored ONLY by the Claude Code CLI; it does NOT apply through the SDK — SDK tool access is controlled by the main `allowedTools` query option.
Without a `canUseTool` callback, anything not in `allowedTools` is denied; pre-approve specific tools via `allowedTools` to control what Skills can do.
`permissionMode: "dontAsk"` denies anything not in `allowedTools` — the non-interactive enforcement mode pairing with an allowlist for unattended/background runs.
To enumerate available Skills in an SDK app, ask Claude directly (e.g. prompt 'What Skills are available?'); it lists them based on cwd and installed plugins.
Test a Skill by prompting a request that matches its description (e.g. 'Extract text from invoice.pdf'); Claude auto-invokes the matching Skill.

Because Skills have no programmatic registration API, an orchestrator must materialize SKILL.md files on disk (and point setting_sources/cwd at them) rather than injecting skills at runtime — the structural constraint shaping SDK-based orchestration.

Claude Agent SDK Subagents guide: how to define and invoke subagents via the `agents` parameter to isolate context, run tasks in parallel, restrict tools, override models/effort, run in background, resume, and scale to dynamic workflows.
Subagents are separate agent instances the main agent spawns to handle focused subtasks, each running in its own fresh conversation to isolate context and apply specialized instructions.

Three ways to create subagents: programmatically via the `agents` parameter in query() options (recommended for SDK), filesystem markdown files in `.claude/agents/`, or the built-in `general-purpose` subagent invocable anytime via the Agent tool with no definition.

The `agents` parameter (a map of name→AgentDefinition) in query() options is the programmatic, recommended way to define subagents for SDK applications; programmatic agents override filesystem agents of the same name.
Claude auto-decides whether to invoke a subagent by matching the task to each subagent's `description` field; clear, specific descriptions are load-bearing for correct delegation.

Context isolation: each subagent runs in its own fresh conversation — intermediate tool calls/results stay inside the subagent and only its final message returns to the parent, keeping the main conversation lean.

Parallelization: multiple subagents run concurrently, so independent subtasks finish in the time of the slowest rather than the sum (e.g. style-checker + security-scanner + test-coverage simultaneously).
Each subagent can carry tailored system prompts with specialized expertise/constraints that would be noise in the main agent's instructions.
Restricting a subagent to specific tools reduces the risk of unintended actions (e.g. a doc-reviewer limited to Read+Grep can analyze but never modify files).
Claude invokes subagents through the `Agent` tool; include `Agent` in `allowedTools` to auto-approve subagent invocations, otherwise they fall through to canUseTool or are denied in dontAsk mode.

AgentDefinition config fields: required `description` and `prompt`; optional `tools`, `disallowedTools`, `model`, `skills`, `memory`, `mcpServers`, `initialPrompt`, `maxTurns`, `background`, `effort`, `permissionMode`.
`model` field overrides the default model per-agent, accepting an alias ('fable','opus','sonnet','haiku','inherit') or full model ID; defaults to the main model if omitted.
`effort` field sets a subagent's reasoning effort level: 'low','medium','high','xhigh','max', or a number.
`maxTurns` field caps the maximum number of agentic turns before the subagent stops.
`background` field, when true, forces a subagent to run as a non-blocking background task regardless of what Claude requests.
`skills` field lists skill names to preload into the agent's context at startup; unlisted skills remain invocable through the Skill tool.
`disallowedTools` removes tools from the agent's set and accepts MCP server-level patterns: `mcp__server`/`mcp__server__*` removes every tool from that server, `mcp__*` removes every MCP tool.
`tools` field restricts the subagent to listed tools; omit it and the agent inherits all available tools (the default).
`initialPrompt` is auto-submitted as the first user turn when the agent runs as the main thread agent, and is ignored when it is invoked as a subagent.

Since Claude Code v2.1.198, subagents run in the background by default: an Agent call omitting `run_in_background` launches a background subagent, and Claude sets `run_in_background: false` when it needs the result before continuing.
Since v2.1.198 a subagent inherits the main session's extended-thinking configuration; on earlier versions extended thinking was disabled inside subagents regardless of the main setting.

Since v2.1.172 subagents can spawn their own subagents, but a subagent five levels below the main agent cannot spawn further; omit `Agent` from its tools or add it to disallowedTools to prevent nesting.

A subagent's context starts fresh: it receives only its own system prompt, the Agent tool's prompt string, inherited tool definitions, and project CLAUDE.md — NOT the parent's conversation history, tool results, or system prompt.
The Agent tool's prompt string is the only content passed parent→subagent, so file paths, error messages, and decisions the subagent needs must be embedded directly in that prompt.
Since v2.1.206 a subagent holding the `SendMessage` tool starts with an auto-added list of the other named agents in the session so it knows which names it can message; a fork does not get the list.

Since v2.1.210 Claude Code scans a subagent's final message for instruction-shaped patterns before the parent reads it — neutralizing control-tag imitation (e.g. `<system-reminder>`), keeping permission-config mentions verbatim, and backslash-escaping `Human:`/`Assistant:` turn markers — without removing or rewording text.

Since v2.1.199, an API error (rate limit/overload/server) that cuts off a foreground subagent returns its partial text output with a note it didn't finish; a subagent that produced no text fails with 'Agent terminated early due to an API error'.
Explicit invocation: mentioning a subagent by name in the prompt (e.g. 'Use the code-reviewer agent to...') bypasses automatic description-matching and directly invokes that subagent.

Dynamic agent configuration: a factory function can build AgentDefinitions at query time from runtime conditions — e.g. picking a more capable model ('opus' vs 'sonnet') for strict/high-stakes reviews.
Detect subagent invocation by checking `tool_use` blocks where name is 'Agent' (or legacy 'Task'); messages originating inside a subagent carry a `parent_tool_use_id` field.
The subagent tool was renamed 'Task'→'Agent' in v2.1.63; current SDKs emit 'Agent' in tool_use blocks but still use 'Task' in system:init tools list and permission_denials, so check both names.

Resume a subagent to continue with full retained history: capture `session_id`, parse `agentId` from the Agent tool result text, then pass `resume: sessionId` in a second query with the agentId in the prompt.
Built-in `Explore` and `Plan` agents are one-shot and return no `agentId`, so resuming requires a custom agent or `general-purpose`; the same agent definition must be passed in both queries.

Subagent transcripts persist independently in separate files: unaffected by main-conversation compaction, resumable after restart via the same session, and auto-cleaned per `cleanupPeriodDays` (default 30 days).
Common tool combinations: read-only analysis (Read,Grep,Glob); test execution (Bash,Read,Grep); code modification (Read,Edit,Write,Grep,Glob); full access (omit tools to inherit all).

For runs coordinating dozens-to-hundreds of agents, use the `Workflow` tool (TS SDK v0.3.149+) which moves orchestration into a runtime-executed script outside the conversation context; add `Workflow` to allowedTools.
Turn-by-turn subagent delegation suits a few tasks per turn; scaling to many agents needs workflows because in-conversation orchestration doesn't hold hundreds of agents.
If Claude answers directly instead of delegating: ensure `Agent` is in allowedTools, prompt the subagent by name explicitly, and write a clear when-to-use description.
On Windows, subagents with very long prompts can fail at the 8191-character command-line limit; keep prompts concise or use filesystem-based agents for complex instructions.
The parent gets the subagent's final message as the Agent tool result but may summarize it; to preserve output verbatim, instruct the main query's prompt/systemPrompt to do so.

Claude Code best-practices guide: patterns for effective agentic coding, centered on the core constraint that the context window fills fast and performance degrades as it fills — covering verification loops, explore-plan-code, prompting, environment config (CLAUDE.md, hooks, skills, subagents), session management, and parallel/autonomous scaling.

Core constraint: Claude's context window holds the entire conversation (every message, file read, command output) and fills fast; LLM performance degrades as it fills — Claude starts forgetting earlier instructions and making more mistakes. Managing context is the single most important resource.

Give Claude a runnable check (test suite, build exit code, linter, fixture diff, browser screenshot) that returns a pass/fail signal Claude reads in-conversation, so it closes the loop itself: does the work, runs the check, reads the result, iterates until it passes.

Why verification matters: Claude stops when work 'looks done'; without a runnable check that is the only signal, and the human becomes the verification loop where every mistake waits to be noticed. A readable pass/fail check closes the loop unattended.

/goal condition — set a verification check as a session-level goal; a separate evaluator re-checks it after every turn and Claude keeps working until it holds. One of the mechanisms that lets an unattended run finish correctly.
Stop hook as deterministic gate — runs your check as a script and blocks the turn from ending until it passes; Claude Code overrides the hook and ends the turn after 8 consecutive blocks.
Verification by second opinion — a verification subagent or dynamic workflow has a FRESH model try to refute the result, so the agent doing the work isn't the one grading it.

Have Claude show EVIDENCE (test output, the command run and its return, a screenshot) rather than asserting success; reviewing evidence is faster than re-running verification and works for unwatched sessions.

Explore-then-plan-then-code workflow (4 phases: Explore, Plan, Implement, Commit) — separate research/planning from implementation to avoid solving the wrong problem; use plan mode for the exploration/planning phases.

Plan mode — Claude reads files and answers questions WITHOUT making changes; Ctrl+G opens the plan in a text editor for direct editing before Claude proceeds. Switch out of plan mode to let Claude code against its plan.

Plan mode adds overhead: skip it for clear small scoped fixes (typo, log line, rename); planning pays off when the approach is uncertain, the change spans multiple files, or the code is unfamiliar. If you could describe the diff in one sentence, skip the plan.

Provide specific context in prompts: reference specific files, state constraints, point to example patterns and sources; more precise instructions mean fewer corrections. Vague prompts are useful only when exploring and able to course-correct.

Provide rich content: reference files with @ (Claude reads before responding), paste/drag images, give URLs (allowlist domains via /permissions), pipe data (cat error.log | claude), or let Claude fetch context itself via Bash/MCP/file reads.

CLAUDE.md — special file read at the start of EVERY conversation; holds Bash commands, code style, workflow rules Claude can't infer from code. Generate a starter with /init; confirm loaded with /context. Loaded every session so keep it concise.

Keep CLAUDE.md concise: for each line ask 'would removing this cause mistakes?' — a bloated CLAUDE.md causes Claude to IGNORE actual instructions as important rules get lost in noise. Add emphasis (IMPORTANT/YOU MUST) to improve adherence; treat it like code and prune regularly.

CLAUDE.md can import other files via @path/to/import syntax and lives in multiple locations: ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md (all sessions), ./CLAUDE.md (team-shared via git), ./CLAUDE.local.md (gitignored personal), plus parent (monorepo) and child directories (loaded on demand).

Reduce permission interruptions three ways: auto mode (classifier model reviews commands), /permissions allowlists (permit specific safe tools like 'npm run lint'), and /sandbox (OS-level filesystem/network isolation) — each reduces prompts while keeping control.

CLI tools (gh, aws, gcloud, sentry-cli) are the most context-efficient way to interact with external services; installing gh avoids unauthenticated API rate limits. Claude can learn unknown CLIs via '<tool> --help' prompts.
MCP servers (claude mcp add) connect external tools — issue trackers, databases, monitoring, Figma designs — to implement features, query data, and automate workflows.

Hooks run scripts automatically at specific points in Claude's workflow; UNLIKE advisory CLAUDE.md instructions, hooks are DETERMINISTIC and guarantee the action happens. Use for actions that must happen every time with zero exceptions; configure in .claude/settings.json, browse with /hooks.

Skills — SKILL.md files in .claude/skills/<name>/ with YAML frontmatter (name, description); extend Claude with project/team/domain knowledge and reusable workflows, applied automatically when relevant or invoked with /skill-name. Loaded on demand so they don't bloat every conversation (unlike CLAUDE.md).
Skill frontmatter 'disable-model-invocation: true' — for workflows with side effects that should be triggered manually only (e.g. /fix-issue with $ARGUMENTS), preventing automatic model invocation.

Custom subagents — defined in .claude/agents/<name>.md with frontmatter (name, description, tools, model e.g. opus); run in their OWN context with their own allowed tools, ideal for tasks reading many files or needing specialized focus without cluttering the main conversation. Invoke explicitly: 'Use a subagent to review this code'.

Subagents run in SEPARATE context windows and report back only summaries — since context is the fundamental constraint, delegating research (reading many files) to subagents keeps the main conversation clean for implementation. One of the most powerful tools available.

Plugins (/plugin marketplace) bundle skills, hooks, subagents, and MCP servers into a single installable unit from the community and Anthropic; e.g. a code-intelligence plugin gives typed-language symbol navigation and automatic error detection after edits.

Let Claude interview you — for larger features start with a minimal prompt and have Claude interview you using the AskUserQuestion tool (technical impl, UI/UX, edge cases, tradeoffs), then write a self-contained SPEC.md; execute it in a FRESH session with clean context.

The most useful specs are self-contained: they name the files/interfaces involved, state what is out of scope, and end with an end-to-end verification step proving the feature works. Precision in the spec pays off more than watching the implementation.

Course-correct early and often via tight feedback loops: Esc stops Claude mid-action (context preserved), Esc+Esc or /rewind opens the rewind menu, 'Undo that' reverts changes, /clear resets context. After 2+ corrections on the same issue, /clear and restart with a better prompt.

Manage context aggressively: /clear frequently between unrelated tasks; auto-compaction triggers near context limits (summarizes code patterns, file states, key decisions); /compact <instructions> for control; Esc+Esc checkpoint summarize; /btw for quick questions that never enter conversation history.

Claude Code automatically compacts conversation history near context limits, preserving important code and decisions while freeing space; customize via CLAUDE.md (e.g. 'when compacting, preserve the full list of modified files and test commands').

Checkpoints — every prompt creates a checkpoint; Claude auto-snapshots files before each change; Esc+Esc or /rewind restores conversation, code, or both. Enables trying risky approaches then rewinding. WARNING: only tracks changes via Claude's file-editing tools, NOT Bash/external changes — not a git replacement.

Resume conversations — Claude Code saves sessions locally; claude --continue picks up the most recent, claude --resume chooses from a list, /rename names sessions (treat them like branches, e.g. 'oauth-migration'), each workstream getting its own persistent context.

Non-interactive/headless mode — claude -p "prompt" runs without an interactive prompt for CI, pre-commit hooks, scripts; still creates a resumable session unless --no-session-persistence. Output formats: plain text, --output-format json (single object with 'result' field), stream-json (one JSON object per line, starting with an init event; use with --verbose).

Run multiple Claude sessions in parallel: worktrees (isolated git checkouts), desktop app (visual multi-session), Claude Code on web (Anthropic-managed cloud VMs), and agent teams (automated coordination with shared tasks, messaging, and a team lead).

Writer/Reviewer pattern — one session implements, a SEPARATE fresh-context session reviews; a fresh context improves code review because Claude won't be biased toward code it just wrote. Same pattern works for tests: one Claude writes tests, another writes code to pass them.

Fan out across files — for large migrations/analyses, loop calling claude -p per file (e.g. 'for file in $(cat files.txt); do claude -p "Migrate $file...Return OK or FAIL" --allowedTools "Edit,Bash(git commit *)"'); test on 2-3 files first, then run at scale. Pipe json output into other commands.

--allowedTools flag scopes/restricts what Claude can do in batch operations (e.g. "Edit,Bash(git commit *)"), which matters when running unattended. --verbose for debugging during development, off in production.

Auto mode (claude --permission-mode auto) — uninterrupted execution where a separate classifier model reviews commands before they run, blocking scope escalation, unknown infrastructure, and hostile-content-driven actions while letting routine work proceed. For -p non-interactive runs it ABORTS if the classifier repeatedly blocks, since there's no user to fall back to.

Adversarial review step — before treating a task done, a reviewer in a FRESH subagent context sees only the diff and criteria (not the reasoning), so it evaluates on its own terms; the implementing session receives gaps directly and fixes+re-reviews without copying between windows. Use bundled /code-review skill or a custom prompt against PLAN.md.

A reviewer prompted to find gaps usually reports some even when work is sound; chasing every finding leads to over-engineering (extra abstraction, defensive code, tests for impossible cases). Tell the reviewer to flag ONLY gaps affecting correctness or stated requirements, treat the rest as optional.

Common failure patterns and fixes: kitchen-sink session (/clear between unrelated tasks), correcting over and over (/clear after 2 failed corrections), over-specified CLAUDE.md (ruthlessly prune / convert to hook), trust-then-verify gap (always provide verification), infinite exploration (scope narrowly or use subagents).

Develop intuition: these patterns are starting points, not rules — sometimes let context accumulate (deep in one problem), skip planning (exploratory task), or use a vague prompt (to see how Claude interprets before constraining). Pay attention to what works and why.

Claude Code 'channels' feature: an MCP server that pushes external events (chat messages, CI results, webhooks, monitoring alerts) into a running Claude Code session so Claude can react while you're away, optionally replying two-way through the same channel.

A channel is an MCP server that PUSHES events into a running Claude Code session (rather than being polled); it can be two-way (Claude reads the event and replies back through the same channel like a chat bridge).
Channel events only arrive WHILE the session is open — for an always-on setup you must run Claude in a background process or persistent terminal.
Unlike integrations that spawn a fresh cloud session or wait to be polled, a channel delivers the event into the session you ALREADY have open, preserving in-progress context and open files.
A channel is installed as a plugin (via /plugin install <name>@claude-plugins-official) and configured with your own credentials; each plugin requires Bun.

claude --channels plugin:<name>@claude-plugins-official — the launch flag that opts specific channel servers into a session; accepts several plugins space-separated; a channel runs ONLY when named here.
When Claude replies through a channel, the terminal shows only the tool call and a confirmation ('sent') — the actual reply text appears on the other platform, not in the terminal.

An inbound channel message reaches the model as a <channel source="plugin:<server>:<name>"> event using the plugin's scoped server name; the terminal shows it as an inbound line like '← fakechat · web: ...'.
Claude responds by calling the channel plugin's `reply` tool; the first reply triggers a terminal permission prompt that must be approved before the answer is delivered to the other platform.
If Claude hits a permission prompt while you're away, the session PAUSES until you respond — a blocking hazard for unattended/background operation.
Channel servers declaring the 'permission relay' capability can forward permission prompts to you (e.g. phone) so you approve/deny remotely, avoiding a stalled unattended session.

--dangerously-skip-permissions (bypassPermissions mode) bypasses most prompts for unattended use, but explicit ask rules, org-set `ask` connector tools, and MCP tools marked `requiresUserInteraction` still prompt; use only in trusted environments.
In non-interactive mode with `-p`, tools needing terminal input (multiple-choice questions, plan-mode approval) are DISABLED so the session never stalls waiting for input.
Every approved channel plugin maintains a sender allowlist — only added sender IDs can push messages; everyone else is silently dropped.

Telegram/Discord bootstrap the allowlist by pairing: message the bot → bot replies with a pairing code → approve it in-session (/telegram:access pair <code>) → sender ID added; iMessage instead bypasses the gate for self-chat.
Being listed in .mcp.json is NOT enough for a server to push messages — it must also be named in --channels for the session.
The sender allowlist also gates permission relay: anyone who can reply through the channel can approve/deny tool use in your session, so only allowlist senders you trust with that authority.
`channelsEnabled` managed setting is the master switch — must be `true` for any channel to deliver messages; when off it blocks all channels including the development flag.
`allowedChannelPlugins` managed setting lists {marketplace, plugin} entries that may register as channels; when set it fully REPLACES the Anthropic default allowlist; requires channelsEnabled: true.

Availability default depends on auth: claude.ai Team/Enterprise BLOCK channels until an Owner enables them; Anthropic Console API-key auth PERMITS by default; Pro/Max users without an org skip the checks entirely.

--dangerously-load-development-channels loads a custom/in-development channel you're building, bypassing the plugin allowlist (but not channelsEnabled); used to test channels during the research preview.

Channels are a research preview: requires Anthropic auth via claude.ai or Console API key; unavailable on Bedrock, Google Vertex/Agent Platform, and Microsoft Foundry; --channels/--dangerously-load-development-channels are hidden from `claude --help` and the flag syntax/protocol may change.

fakechat — officially supported demo channel running a localhost chat UI (http://localhost:8787) with no auth or external service, for testing the plugin push→reply flow before connecting a real platform.

Research-preview supported channels are Telegram, Discord, and iMessage (each a Bun plugin); iMessage is macOS-only, reads ~/Library/Messages/chat.db directly and replies via AppleScript, needing no bot token.
Webhook-receiver pattern: a webhook from CI, an error tracker, or a deploy pipeline arrives in the session where Claude already has your files open and remembers what you were debugging.

Channels vs adjacent features: Claude Code on the web (fresh cloud sandbox, async delegation), Claude in Slack (spawns web session from @mention), standard MCP server (pull/query only, nothing pushed), Remote Control (drive local session from phone); channels uniquely PUSH external events into an already-running local session.
Scheduled tasks are the timer-based alternative — poll on a schedule instead of reacting to pushed channel events.

Claude Code 'Common workflows' page — step-by-step recipes for codebase exploration, bug fixing, refactoring, testing, PRs, docs, plus resuming conversations, parallel worktree sessions, plan mode, subagent delegation, scheduling, and piping Claude into scripts.

Delegate research to subagents: prompt 'use a subagent to investigate <X>' — the subagent reads files in its OWN context window and reports back only a summary, keeping the main conversation's context clean.

Rationale: exploring a large codebase fills the main context with file reads; delegating to a subagent means only the findings come back, not the raw reads — context-window preservation is the design motive.
Custom subagents can be defined with their own tools and prompts (see /docs/en/sub-agents) — the extension point for building specialized delegated agents.

claude --worktree <name> — creates an isolated parallel session on a separate git-worktree checkout/branch from an existing commit; run with a different name in another terminal for concurrent non-colliding edits.
Worktrees require at least one commit: in a repo with no commits, claude --worktree fails with 'Failed to resolve base branch "HEAD": git rev-parse failed'.
Background agents / agent-view: monitor multiple parallel sessions from one screen instead of separate terminals (see /docs/en/agent-view).
Plan mode: claude --permission-mode plan — Claude reads files and proposes a plan but makes NO edits until approved; status bar shows '⏸ plan mode on'.
Shift+Tab mid-session cycles permission modes in order default → acceptEdits → plan; used to enter plan mode without restarting.
Rationale: plan mode exists for changes you want to review BEFORE they touch disk — a human-approval gate between proposal and file mutation.
Pipe Claude into scripts: claude -p '<prompt>' runs non-interactively for CI, pre-commit hooks, and batch processing; stdin/stdout behave like any Unix tool (e.g. `git log | claude -p 'summarize'`).
Non-interactive (headless) mode supports output formats, permission flags, and fan-out patterns (see /docs/en/headless) — the surface for scripted/parallel automation.

claude --continue resumes the most recent session in the current directory; if none exists it prints 'No conversation found to continue' and exits. --resume picks from a list; /resume works inside a running session.
Claude Code saves every conversation locally so a task can span multiple sittings without re-explaining context.

Four scheduling options differ by where the task runs: Routines (Anthropic infra, runs when computer off, API/GitHub triggers), Desktop scheduled tasks (local files/tools), GitHub Actions (CI pipeline, repo events/cron), /loop (current CLI session, quick polling).
/loop runs in the current CLI session for quick polling; tasks STOP when a new conversation starts, and --resume/--continue restore unexpired ones.
Rationale for scheduled-task prompts: the task runs autonomously and CANNOT ask clarifying questions, so prompts must be explicit about what success looks like and what to do with results.
@ references inject file/dir content without waiting for Claude to read: @file includes full file content, @dir gives a directory listing, @server:resource fetches from a connected MCP server.
An @ file reference also pulls the CLAUDE.md in that file's directory and parent directories into context.
MCP resources are referenced with @server:resource format (e.g. @github:repos/owner/repo/issues) to fetch live data from connected MCP servers.
Creating a PR via `gh pr create` auto-links the session to that PR; `claude --from-pr 1234` reopens the session picker filtered to PR-linked sessions.
When generating tests Claude examines existing test files to match the project's style, frameworks, and assertion patterns, and can identify edge/error/boundary cases the author missed.
Claude has built-in access to its own latest Claude Code documentation (regardless of installed version) and answers capability/limitation questions from it; /powerup gives interactive lessons.
Claude reads files fresh on each tool call, so edits made in another application are seen the next time that file is read; .claude/ and CLAUDE.md coexist with other tools' config dirs.
Images enter a conversation via drag-and-drop, Ctrl+V (Cmd+V in iTerm2 on macOS), or a file path; Claude analyzes diagrams, screenshots, mockups and generates code (CSS/HTML) from visual content.
A code-intelligence plugin for the language gives Claude precise 'go to definition' / 'find references' navigation when locating relevant code.
Claude Code /goal: sets a completion condition so Claude keeps working across turns without per-turn prompting, with a small fast model checking after each turn whether the condition is met.
/goal <condition> sets a session-scoped completion condition; Claude keeps starting new turns until met, requires Claude Code v2.1.139+, and one goal can be active per session.
After each turn a small fast model evaluator checks whether the condition holds; if not, Claude starts another turn instead of returning control; the goal clears automatically once met.

Goals suit substantial work with a verifiable end state: API-migration until call sites compile+tests pass, design-doc acceptance criteria, splitting a file under a size budget, draining a labeled issue backlog.
/loop starts each next turn when a time interval elapses and stops when you stop it or Claude decides work is done — interval-driven, unlike /goal's condition-driven turns.

A Stop hook fires after every turn like /goal but lives in the settings file, applies to every session in its scope, and can run a script (deterministic) or a prompt (model-evaluated); /goal is a session-scoped shortcut you type once.

Auto mode approves tool calls within a single turn but never starts a new turn; /goal adds a separate fresh-model evaluator that decides per-turn completion — the two are complementary (auto removes per-tool prompts, /goal removes per-turn prompts).
Setting a goal immediately starts a turn with the condition itself as the directive (no separate prompt needed); a new /goal replaces any already-active goal.
A goal does NOT change permissions — in default mode Claude still asks before disallowed tool calls; pair /goal with auto mode to let goal turns run unattended.
After each turn the evaluator returns a short yes/no reason; a 'no' is passed to Claude as guidance for the next turn, and the most recent reason appears in the status view and transcript.
Write conditions as something Claude's own output can demonstrate: the evaluator judges only what's surfaced in the conversation, does NOT run commands or read files independently.

A durable condition has one measurable end state (test result/exit code/file count/empty queue), a stated check (e.g. 'npm test exits 0'), and constraints that must not change (e.g. 'no other test file modified').
A /goal condition can be up to 4,000 characters.
To bound runtime, include a turn/time clause in the condition (e.g. 'or stop after 20 turns'); Claude reports progress against it each turn and the evaluator judges it from the conversation.

/goal with no argument shows current state: condition, elapsed time, turns evaluated, current token spend, and the evaluator's most recent reason (turn count + reason appear after the first evaluation).
/goal clear removes an active goal before its condition is met (aliases: stop, off, reset, none, cancel); /clear also removes any active goal; prints 'Goal cleared:' or 'No goal set'.

A goal active at session end is restored on --resume/--continue: the condition carries over but turn count, timer, and token-spend baseline all reset; an already-achieved or cleared goal is not restored.
/goal works in non-interactive/headless mode, the desktop app, and Remote Control; 'claude -p "/goal ..."' runs the whole loop to completion in a single invocation.

In headless mode default text output prints nothing until the condition is met (a long goal looks stuck); add --output-format stream-json --verbose to emit each message; Ctrl+C interrupts before completion.
/goal is a wrapper around a session-scoped prompt-based Stop hook: each turn's condition + conversation are sent to the configured small fast model, which returns a yes/no decision and short reason.
The evaluator uses the configured 'small fast model' (defaults to Haiku) on whichever provider the session uses; it calls no tools so it can only judge what Claude already surfaced.
Evaluation tokens are billed on the small fast model and are typically negligible compared to main-turn spend.

/goal runs only in trust-accepted workspaces (the evaluator is part of the hooks system) and is unavailable when disableAllHooks is set at any level or allowManagedHooksOnly is set in managed settings; the command explains why rather than silently no-op'ing.
For work independent of any open session (nightly tests, morning triage) use cloud routines or desktop scheduled tasks rather than session-keeping approaches like /goal, /loop, or Stop hooks.

Claude Code Hooks reference: user-defined shell commands, HTTP endpoints, MCP tools, LLM prompts, or subagents that execute automatically at lifecycle points — covers event schemas, matchers, JSON input/output, exit codes, decision control, and async/background/prompt/agent hooks

A hook is a handler (shell command, HTTP endpoint, MCP tool, LLM prompt, or subagent) that fires at a specific point in Claude Code's lifecycle; input arrives on stdin (command) or as the POST body (HTTP), and the handler can inspect, act, and return a decision

Hook events fire at three cadences: once per session (SessionStart/SessionEnd), once per turn (UserPromptSubmit/Stop/StopFailure), and on every tool call inside the agentic loop (PreToolUse/PostToolUse)
Hook configuration has three nesting levels: choose a hook EVENT, add a MATCHER GROUP to filter when it fires, then define one or more hook HANDLERS to run when matched

Hook scope depends on where it's defined: ~/.claude/settings.json (all projects), .claude/settings.json (project, committable), .claude/settings.local.json (project, gitignored), managed policy settings (org-wide), plugin hooks/hooks.json, or skill/agent frontmatter (while component active)

The matcher field filters when hooks fire: '*'/''/'omitted' match all; letters/digits/_/-/space/,/| are exact string(s) with |-or-, alternatives; any other char makes it an unanchored JavaScript RegExp (anchor with ^...$ for whole-string)

The optional `if` field uses permission-rule syntax to filter a handler by tool name AND arguments together, e.g. "Bash(git *)" or "Edit(*.ts)"; only evaluated on tool events (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, PostToolUseFailure, PermissionRequest, PermissionDenied), holds exactly one rule (no &&/||)
The `if` Bash filter is best-effort and FAILS OPEN — it runs the hook regardless of pattern when the Bash command can't be parsed; use the permission system, not a hook, to enforce a hard allow/deny

Five hook handler types: command (shell, stdin+exit codes), http (POST to URL), mcp_tool (call a connected MCP server tool), prompt (single-turn LLM yes/no decision), agent (spawn a subagent with tool access to verify — experimental)
All matching hooks run in PARALLEL and identical handlers are deduplicated automatically (command hooks by command+args, HTTP hooks by URL)
Handler `timeout` defaults: 600s for command/http/mcp_tool, 30s for prompt, 60s for agent; UserPromptSubmit lowers command/http/mcp_tool to 30s and MessageDisplay to 10s

The `effort` input field (object with `level`: low/medium/high/xhigh/max) reports the active effort level for the turn — downgraded to what the model actually supports; Ultracode reports as xhigh; also exposed as $CLAUDE_EFFORT env var; present for tool-context events (PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, SubagentStop)

Hook exit codes: 0 = success (stdout parsed as JSON output, only on exit 0); 2 = blocking error (stdout/JSON ignored, stderr fed to Claude, effect per-event); any other code = non-blocking error, execution continues

Exit code 2 blockability varies by event: PreToolUse blocks the tool call, Stop/SubagentStop prevent stopping, TaskCreated/TaskCompleted roll back, TeammateIdle keeps teammate working, PreCompact blocks compaction; Post* and SessionStart/Setup/SubagentStart cannot block

JSON output on exit 0 gives finer control than exit codes: universal fields (continue, stopReason, suppressOutput, systemMessage, terminalSequence), top-level decision/reason, and nested hookSpecificOutput (requires hookEventName); output strings capped at 10,000 chars
`continue: false` stops Claude processing entirely after the hook runs, taking precedence over any event-specific decision field; `stopReason` message is shown to the user, not Claude

`additionalContext` (inside hookSpecificOutput) injects a string into Claude's context as a system reminder at the point the hook fired; used for dynamic environment state, conditional rules, or external data
Prefer additionalContext for information that changes (branch, deploy target, CI results); prefer static CLAUDE.md for instructions that never change since it loads without running a script

Decision control differs per event: top-level `decision: block`+reason (UserPromptSubmit, PostToolUse, Stop, etc.), PreToolUse uses hookSpecificOutput.permissionDecision, PermissionRequest uses decision.behavior, TaskCreated/TaskCompleted/TeammateIdle use exit 2 or continue:false

PreToolUse decision control returns hookSpecificOutput.permissionDecision with four outcomes — allow (skip prompt), deny (block), ask (confirm), defer (resume later) — plus updatedInput to rewrite tool args before execution; multi-hook precedence is deny > defer > ask > allow

The Agent tool (spawns a subagent) tool_input carries `prompt` (task), `description`, `subagent_type` (e.g. Explore), and optional `model` alias to override the default — a hook can inspect or rewrite these at PreToolUse

On PostToolUse a completed Agent call's tool_response carries per-subagent cost telemetry: totalTokens, totalDurationMs, totalToolUseCount, usage (input/output/cache tokens), resolvedModel, and modelsUsed — read these from a hook to record per-subagent cost

resolvedModel names the model the subagent actually started on, which can differ from the requested tool_input.model (e.g. availableModels override); modelsUsed lists models in order when swapped mid-run (v2.1.212+)

As of v2.1.198 subagents run in the BACKGROUND by default; a background/backgrounded Agent call's tool_response returns immediately with status 'async_launched', agentId, outputFile, and resolvedModel but NO usage fields (foreground completed calls return 'completed' with full usage)

permissionDecision 'defer' (honored only in claude -p non-interactive mode) pauses Claude at a single tool call; the process exits with stop_reason 'tool_deferred' and deferred_tool_use payload, and the calling SDK app resumes via claude -p --resume <session-id>, returning the answer in updatedInput

SubagentStart fires when a subagent is spawned via the Agent tool; matches on agent_type (built-in like general-purpose/Explore/Plan, custom frontmatter name, or plugin-scoped my-plugin:reviewer); cannot block but can inject additionalContext into the subagent's context

SubagentStop fires when a subagent finishes; receives last_assistant_message (subagent's final text without parsing the transcript), agent_transcript_path (nested subagents/ folder), plus parent-scoped background_tasks/session_crons; decision:block keeps the subagent running with reason as its next instruction

Stop and SubagentStop provide last_assistant_message with the final response text; use it instead of reading transcript_path, which is written asynchronously and may lag the in-memory conversation and not yet include the current turn

TaskCreated fires when a task is created via TaskCreate (agent teams); receives task_id, task_subject, task_description, teammate_name; exit 2 rolls back creation and feeds stderr to the model, or JSON continue:false stops the teammate entirely — enforce naming/description conventions

TaskCompleted fires when a task is marked completed (via TaskUpdate) or a teammate finishes with in-progress tasks; exit 2 blocks completion and feeds stderr back — enforce completion criteria like passing tests or lint before a task can close

Stop fires when the main agent finishes responding (not on user interrupt; API errors fire StopFailure instead); decision:block with a reason prevents Claude from stopping and continues the conversation

Stop-hook loop protection: stop_hook_active is true when Claude is already continuing from a stop hook, and Claude Code overrides the hook and ends the turn after 8 consecutive blocks — check stop_hook_active to avoid blocking on a condition that never resolves

Stop/SubagentStop input includes background_tasks (in-flight tasks: shell, subagent, monitor, workflow, teammate, cloud session, MCP task) and session_crons (scheduled wakeups from CronCreate/ScheduleWakeup//loop) so hooks distinguish 'session done' from 'paused waiting for background work'
The /goal command is a built-in shortcut for a session-scoped prompt-based Stop hook — makes Claude keep working until a condition holds without writing hook configuration

StopFailure fires instead of Stop when the turn ends due to an API error; output and exit code are ignored; matches on error type (rate_limit, overloaded, authentication_failed, billing_error, model_not_found, max_output_tokens, etc.) — use for logging, alerts, recovery
TeammateIdle fires when an agent-team teammate is about to go idle; exit 2 (or a blocking prompt hook) prevents it going idle so it continues working, and continue:false stops it entirely

Prompt hooks (type: prompt) send $ARGUMENTS (hook input JSON) to a Claude model for single-turn evaluation; model defaults to a fast model; the LLM returns {ok: true|false, reason}; ok:false produces decision:block, with continueOnBlock:true feeding the reason back and continuing instead of stopping the turn

Agent hooks (type: agent, EXPERIMENTAL) spawn a subagent that uses Read/Grep/Glob over up to 50 turns to investigate real files/test output before returning {ok: true|false}; default timeout 60s; for production prefer command hooks

Agent hooks are worth the cost only when verification requires inspecting actual files or test output, not just evaluating the hook input data alone; they are experimental and may change, so prefer deterministic command hooks in production

Setting async:true (command hooks only) runs a hook in the BACKGROUND without blocking Claude; async hooks CANNOT block or return decisions (the triggering action already completed); output/additionalContext is delivered on the next conversation turn (or when idle ends)

asyncRewake:true runs in background and wakes Claude immediately on exit code 2 (even when idle), showing the hook's stderr (or stdout) as a system reminder so Claude can react to a long-running background failure

SessionStart runs on every new/resumed session (matchers startup/resume/clear/compact/fork); only command and mcp_tool types; can return additionalContext, initialUserMessage (first turn in -p mode), sessionTitle, watchPaths, and reloadSkills; keep fast

reloadSkills:true makes Claude Code re-scan skill/command directories after SessionStart hooks complete, so a hook that installs/updates skills makes them available in the same session (otherwise they'd only appear next session)

SessionStart/Setup/CwdChanged/FileChanged hooks get CLAUDE_ENV_FILE — a path where export statements (appended with >>) persist environment variables into all subsequent Bash commands Claude runs during the session

PostToolBatch fires after a full batch of parallel tool calls resolves, before the next model call; exit 2 stops the agentic loop before the next model call — a control point over the whole parallel batch

Hooks can be declared in skill/subagent frontmatter, scoped to the component's lifetime and cleaned up when it finishes; for subagents, Stop hooks auto-convert to SubagentStop; the `once` field (run once per session) is honored only for skill-frontmatter hooks

Claude Code model configuration page: how to select and constrain models (aliases, full IDs), tune effort/reasoning, control models for subagents/workflows, configure fallback chains, extended context, and prompt caching across Anthropic API and third-party providers
The `model` setting accepts a model alias OR a full model name (Anthropic API full name, Bedrock inference-profile ARN, Foundry deployment name, or Google Agent Platform version name)

Model aliases (default/best/fable/sonnet/opus/haiku/sonnet[1m]/opus[1m]/opusplan) select model settings without version numbers; they point to the recommended version per provider and update over time
`best` alias uses Fable 5 where the org has access, otherwise the latest Opus — the top-capability selector for hardest work
`haiku` alias selects the fast/efficient Haiku model for simple tasks; also drives background functionality via ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_HAIKU_MODEL
`opusplan` alias: uses `opus` during plan mode for reasoning/architecture, then auto-switches to `sonnet` for execution/code-generation; `opusplan[1m]` forces 1M context in both phases

opusplan pairs Opus's reasoning for planning with Sonnet's efficiency for execution — a hybrid that switches at the plan/execution boundary (vs the advisor tool which consults a second model mid-task)

Claude Fable 5 is the most capable Claude Code model for tasks larger than a single sitting: sustains long autonomous sessions, investigates before acting, and self-verifies more than smaller models; not the default, select with `/model fable`

To get the most from Fable 5: describe the outcome not the steps (and set a goal), hand it ambiguous root-cause/architecture problems, skip verification reminders (it self-verifies), and size up larger tasks it can hold without losing the thread

Model-setting precedence (highest first): `/model` in-session or `--model` flag, then ANTHROPIC_MODEL env var, then `model` field in settings; as of v2.1.153 `/model` saves the choice as the default for new sessions

`--model` flag and ANTHROPIC_MODEL apply only to the launched session; to run different models in different terminals concurrently, launch each with its own `--model` rather than switching with `/model`

Resumed sessions (--resume/--continue//resume) keep the model in the saved transcript regardless of current `model` setting, unless it's retired/excluded; a `--model` or ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_*_MODEL at relaunch still overrides the restored model

`availableModels` (managed/policy settings) restricts which models users can select everywhere: main session, alias resolution, subagents, skills/commands, advisor, background agents; entries match a family (`sonnet`), version prefix, or full ID

On Anthropic API / Claude Platform on AWS, a family alias (opus/sonnet/haiku/fable) under an allowlist resolves to the NEWEST permitted version of its family; e.g. with `["sonnet","claude-opus-4-6"]`, `opus` selects Opus 4.6 and shows a substitution notice

`enforceAvailableModels: true` (with non-empty availableModels, managed settings) extends the allowlist to the Default option; an empty availableModels array never engages Default enforcement so users can't be locked out of every model

Organization default model (Enterprise): admin sets a Default for the org or per role from claude.ai console; read once at startup, any explicit model selection (--model/ANTHROPIC_MODEL/settings) takes precedence unless admin enables override-user-selection

Organization model restrictions (Enterprise): admins disable individual models in the claude.ai console; server-enforced at session creation, org-wide or per-role; Haiku is always available and can't be disabled so every member keeps one usable model

Organization effort limits (Enterprise): admins cap max effort level per model per role; levels above the cap aren't offered and `--effort`/`/effort` above it run at the cap; least-restrictive cap applies across roles

`default` model setting resolves per account type: Opus 4.8 on Max/Team Premium/Enterprise-PAYG/Anthropic API/AWS/Bedrock/Google; Sonnet 5 on Pro/Team Standard/Enterprise seats; Sonnet 4.5 on Microsoft Foundry — overridden by an org default when set

Fallback model chains: when the primary is overloaded/unavailable/returns a non-retryable server error, Claude Code switches to a fallback (auth/billing/rate-limit/size/transport errors never trigger it); the switch lasts the current turn only

Configure fallback with `--fallback-model sonnet,haiku` (comma-separated, per-session, takes precedence) or `fallbackModel` array in settings; capped at 3 models after dedup, each entry a name/alias, `"default"` expands to the default model
Fallback won't pick a model with a smaller context window than the primary during compaction, since summarizing in a smaller window would cut off part of the conversation first

Automatic model fallback: Fable 5 safety classifiers (cybersecurity/biology) flag a request → Claude Code re-runs it on the provider's default Opus (Opus 4.8 on API) and continues there; `/model fable` returns to Fable 5; if the Opus target is allowlist-blocked the request ends in a refusal

Auto-fallback can trigger on the first request because it carries workspace context (CLAUDE.md, git status); `claude --safe-mode` disables customizations (CLAUDE.md/skills/MCP/hooks) to test whether they're the trigger

`/config` → turn off 'switch models when a message is flagged' to pause on each flagged request with two options (switch to Opus, or edit-and-retry on Fable 5); non-interactive/SDK sessions end the turn with a refusal instead

Effort levels control adaptive reasoning (model decides whether/how much to think per step): low, medium, high, xhigh, max — lower is faster/cheaper, higher is deeper reasoning; effort scale is calibrated per model so a level name isn't equal across models

Effort support by model: Fable 5 / Sonnet 5 / Opus 4.8 / Opus 4.7 support low..max; Opus 4.6 & Sonnet 4.6 support low/medium/high/max (no xhigh); unsupported level falls back to highest supported at-or-below; default effort is `high` (`xhigh` on Opus 4.7)

Set effort via `/effort [level|auto]`, `/model` slider, `--effort` flag, CLAUDE_CODE_EFFORT_LEVEL env var, `effortLevel` setting (low/medium/high/xhigh only), or `effort` frontmatter in skills/subagents; precedence: env var > configured level > model default

`ultracode` is a Claude Code setting (not a model effort level): sends `xhigh` to the model AND has Claude orchestrate dynamic workflows for substantive tasks; session-only, not accepted by effortLevel setting or CLAUDE_CODE_EFFORT_LEVEL

`ultrathink` anywhere in a prompt requests deeper reasoning for that one turn via an in-context instruction without changing session effort; 'think'/'think hard'/'think more' are NOT recognized keywords and pass through as ordinary text

Adaptive reasoning makes thinking optional per step; Fable 5, Sonnet 5, and Opus 4.7+ always use it (fixed-budget mode and CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_ADAPTIVE_THINKING don't apply); Opus 4.6 / Sonnet 4.6 can revert to a fixed budget via MAX_THINKING_TOKENS

Extended thinking (reasoning emitted before responding): effort level is the primary control on adaptive models; toggle with Option/Alt+T, set default via alwaysThinkingEnabled, MAX_THINKING_TOKENS=0 disables (except Fable 5); billed for all thinking tokens even when collapsed/redacted

Extended context: Fable 5, Sonnet 5, Opus 4.6+, Sonnet 4.6 support a 1M-token window for large codebases; select via `[1m]` alias/name suffix; 1M uses standard pricing with no premium beyond 200K; CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_1M_CONTEXT=1 removes 1M variants
On the Anthropic API Sonnet 5 always runs the 1M window (no 200K variant, no [1m] suffix, no credits); sessions auto-compact at ~967K tokens by default, tunable via CLAUDE_CODE_AUTO_COMPACT_WINDOW

CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL sets the model for ALL subagents, agent teams, and workflow-run agents (alias or full name); overrides the per-invocation Agent-tool `model` param and subagent frontmatter `model`; set to `inherit` for normal model resolution
ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_HAIKU_MODEL sets the model for the `haiku` alias AND for background functionality/background token usage; ANTHROPIC_SMALL_FAST_MODEL is deprecated in favor of it

ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_{FABLE,OPUS,SONNET,HAIKU}_MODEL each pin a full model name that its alias resolves to (OPUS also backs opusplan in plan mode, SONNET backs opusplan in execution); FABLE var also identifies Fable 5 for auto-fallback on third-party providers

ANTHROPIC_CUSTOM_MODEL_OPTION adds one custom entry to the `/model` picker (with optional _NAME/_DESCRIPTION) without replacing built-in aliases; validation is skipped so any provider-accepted string works; must be listed in availableModels when an allowlist is set

`modelOverrides` setting maps individual Anthropic model IDs to provider-specific strings (Bedrock ARN / Agent Platform version / Foundry deployment) for per-version routing, governance, and cost allocation; evaluated for --model/env-var IDs as of v2.1.200

On Bedrock/Google Agent Platform/Foundry/Claude Platform on AWS, pin model versions via ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_*_MODEL to version-specific IDs so admins control when users move to a new model; unpinned aliases can lag or point to an unavailable default

On third-party providers set ANTHROPIC_DEFAULT_*_MODEL_SUPPORTED_CAPABILITIES (effort, xhigh_effort, max_effort, thinking, adaptive_thinking, interleaved_thinking) to declare which features a provider-specific model ID supports, since ID pattern-matching may not detect them
Claude Code auto-uses prompt caching to cut cost/latency; disable globally with DISABLE_PROMPT_CACHING=1 or per-tier with DISABLE_PROMPT_CACHING_{HAIKU,SONNET,OPUS,FABLE}=1 (global takes precedence)

Each effort level trades token spend against capability: `low` for short/latency-sensitive non-intelligence-sensitive tasks, `medium` for cost-sensitive, `high` (default) balances, `xhigh` deeper, `max` for demanding tasks but risks overthinking/diminishing returns — test before adopting broadly

Claude Code 'What's new' weekly dev digest (weeks 13-29, Mar-Jul 2026, v2.1.83-v2.1.212) summarizing notable features: models, subagents/workflows, auto mode, hooks, effort levels, artifacts, and background execution
Subagents run in the BACKGROUND by default (Week 27) so Claude keeps working while they run — foundational for multi-agent orchestration
Sub-agents can spawn their own sub-agents (Week 24); background chains are CAPPED at five levels deep
Dynamic workflows (Week 22) orchestrate dozens to hundreds of subagents from a script that Claude writes — programmatic fan-out orchestration
/fork (Week 29) copies your conversation into a NEW BACKGROUND SESSION while you keep working in the original
Background subagents (Week 26) surface permission prompts in the MAIN session instead of auto-denying — prevents silent orchestration stalls
Background sessions (Week 21) appear in /resume and stay alive when PINNED
fallbackModel config (Week 24) sets up to THREE fallback models tried in order — resilience for orchestrated runs
Effort levels: /effort slider dials effort; xhigh is the recommended setting for most coding work (Week 16); Opus 4.8 defaults to high effort with /effort xhigh for hardest tasks (Week 22)
Hooks see the active effort level (Week 19) via effort.level and the $CLAUDE_EFFORT env var — effort-aware orchestration control
Conditional 'if' hooks (Week 13) — hooks can fire conditionally
Claude Opus 4.8 (Week 22): default model for Max/Team Premium/Enterprise PAYG/API accounts, high effort by default, fast mode at $10/$50 per MTok
Claude Opus 4.7 (Week 16): default on Max and Team Premium, introduced the xhigh effort level and interactive /effort slider
Claude Sonnet 5 (Week 27): default for Pro/Team Standard/Enterprise seats, top-tier coding+tool use at Sonnet pricing, native 1M-token context window, adaptive thinking on by default

Auto mode (Week 13, research preview): a CLASSIFIER handles permission prompts so safe actions run uninterrupted and risky ones are blocked — middle ground between approving everything and --dangerously-skip-permissions
Auto mode safety guards: blocks destructive git commands (Week 25), blocks transcript tampering, asks before rm -rf on unresolved variables (Week 28), safer edits prompt in acceptEdits mode (Week 23)
Auto mode on third-party providers (Week 23/29): Amazon Bedrock, Google Cloud Agent Platform, Microsoft Foundry for Opus 4.7/4.8; no opt-in variable needed as of Week 29
Auto mode HARD DENY rules (Week 19) block actions unconditionally regardless of allow exceptions
Deny/ask rules match tool PARAMETERS (Week 25) with Tool(param:value) syntax, e.g. Agent(model:opus) — fine-grained permission control over subagent models
/goal (Week 20) keeps Claude working across turns until a COMPLETION CONDITION holds — persistent goal-driven loop
/loop (Week 15) runs a prompt/command on a recurring interval; SELF-PACES when the interval is omitted
Monitor tool (Week 15) streams background events into the conversation so Claude can tail logs and REACT LIVE to long-running work
/usage (Week 21) breaks down what drives plan limits by SKILL, SUBAGENT, PLUGIN, and MCP server — cost attribution for orchestration
/code-review command (Week 21) reports correctness bugs; /ultrareview (Week 17) runs a fleet of cloud bug-hunting agents returning findings to CLI/Desktop
Agent view / 'claude agents' (Week 20) opens one screen for every session showing what's running, blocked on you, or done; rows show a colored state word and classifier-written headline (Week 28)
claude mcp login/logout (Week 26) authenticates a configured MCP server from the shell and clears stored credentials — non-interactive MCP auth for automation
Per-tool MCP result-size override up to 500K (Week 14) — controls how much MCP output enters context
/config key=value (Week 25) sets any setting from the prompt, in -p mode, and from Remote Control
Routines (Week 16) on Claude Code web fire templated CLOUD AGENTS from a schedule, GitHub event, or API call
worktree.baseRef (Week 19) chooses whether new worktrees branch from the remote default or local HEAD
/cd (Week 24) moves the session to a new working directory mid-conversation WITHOUT rebuilding the prompt cache
--safe-mode (Week 24) starts Claude Code with all customizations disabled for troubleshooting
Computer use (Week 14, CLI research preview): Claude opens native apps, clicks UI, and verifies changes — best for closing the loop on things only a GUI can verify
Artifacts (Week 25): turn a session's output into a live shareable claude.ai page that updates in place; can call each viewer's own MCP connectors for live data/actions (Week 29)
WHY subagents default to background: so Claude keeps working while they run — parallel progress instead of blocking on each delegated task

WHY auto mode exists: to provide a safe middle ground between approving every permission prompt and the all-or-nothing --dangerously-skip-permissions, letting safe actions run uninterrupted while risky ones are blocked
WHY fallbackModel: configuring up to three ordered fallbacks provides resilience when a primary model is unavailable during long autonomous runs

Claude Code dynamic workflows: a JavaScript script Claude writes and a runtime executes in the background to orchestrate dozens-to-hundreds of subagents at scale for audits, migrations, and cross-checked research.

A dynamic workflow is a JS script (plain JavaScript with top-level await) that orchestrates subagents at scale; Claude writes it for the described task and a runtime executes it in the background while the session stays responsive.

Dynamic workflows require Claude Code v2.1.154+, are on all paid plans (Pro/Max/Team/Enterprise), Anthropic API, Amazon Bedrock, Google Cloud Agent Platform, and Microsoft Foundry; on Pro enable via the Dynamic workflows row in /config.
