A workflow moves the plan into code: the script holds the loop, branching, and intermediate results, so Claude's context holds only the final answer — unlike subagents/skills/agent-teams where Claude is the turn-by-turn orchestrator and every result lands in a context window.

Moving the plan into code lets a workflow apply repeatable quality patterns beyond scale: independent agents can adversarially review each other's findings before reporting, or draft a plan from several angles and weigh them, yielding a more trustworthy result than a single pass.

Orchestration primitives compared: Subagents (Claude spawns workers, results in context, few per turn), Skills (instructions Claude follows), Agent teams (lead agent supervises peer sessions via a shared task list), Workflows (a script the runtime executes, dozens-to-hundreds of agents, resumable).

/deep-research <question> — bundled built-in workflow that fans out web searches across several angles, fetches and cross-checks sources, votes on each claim, and returns a cited report with unsurvived claims filtered out; requires the WebSearch tool.
As of v2.1.196, when /deep-research verifier agents can't check a claim (e.g. rate limit or API error), the report lists it as unverified instead of counting it as refuted.

/workflows lists running and completed workflows; select a run and press Enter to open its progress view showing each phase with agent count, token total, and elapsed time; drill into a phase/agent to read its prompt, recent tool calls, and result.

Progress-view controls: ↑/↓ select, Enter/→ drill in, Esc/← back out, j/k scroll, f filter agents by status (v2.1.186+), p pause/resume, x stop agent or whole workflow, r restart a running agent, s save the run's script as a command.

Include the keyword `ultracode` in a typed prompt to run a single task as a workflow without changing session effort; natural-language 'use a workflow'/'run a workflow' works too. Before v2.1.160 the literal keyword was `workflow`.

A workflow started via the keyword runs inside the session's existing permission mode and sandboxing; the keyword only chooses HOW Claude structures the work (script vs turn-by-turn), not what permissions apply.

The ultracode keyword is an opt-in only in a prompt YOU type (interactive prompt, IDE panel, Remote Control, or Agent SDK input stamped origin {kind:'human'}); it does NOT trigger from -p, unstamped SDK sends, scheduled task prompts, or webhook/PR-comment payloads (as of v2.1.210).

Ultracode (/effort ultracode, or launch `claude --effort ultracode`, v2.1.203+) combines `xhigh` reasoning effort with automatic workflow orchestration: Claude plans a workflow for every substantive task, so each request uses more tokens and takes longer.

With ultracode on, a single request can become several workflows in a row — one to understand the code, one to make the change, one to verify it — and it applies to every task in the session until reset by a new session or /effort high.

Before a run, the CLI shows planned phases with options Yes-run-it / Yes-and-don't-ask-again-for-<name>-in-<path> / View-raw-script / No; Ctrl+G opens the script in an editor, Tab lets you adjust the prompt.

Launch-prompt frequency by permission mode: Default/accept-edits prompt every run unless don't-ask-again; Auto prompts first launch only (records consent, skipped when ultracode on); Bypass-permissions/`claude -p`/Agent SDK never prompt and start immediately.

Regardless of session mode, workflow-spawned subagents ALWAYS run in acceptEdits mode and inherit your tool allowlist; file edits are auto-approved. Only the launch prompt is governed by session permission mode.

Shell commands, web fetches, and MCP tools NOT in your allowlist can still prompt mid-run; add needed commands to the allowlist before a long run. Under `claude -p`/Agent SDK there is no one to prompt, so tool calls follow configured rules without interactive confirmation.

Save a run's script as a reusable command: /workflows → select run → press s → Tab toggles save location: `.claude/workflows/` (project, shared) or `~/.claude/workflows/` (personal, respects CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR); runs as /<name> in future sessions.

In a monorepo (v2.1.178+) saving to project location writes to the closest existing `.claude/workflows/` between cwd and repo root; workflows load from every such dir along the path, and the one closest to cwd wins on name collision; a project workflow beats a personal one of the same name.

A saved workflow accepts input via the `args` parameter, read inside the script as a global named `args`; Claude passes structured data (arrays/objects usable directly without parsing). If omitted, `args` is undefined.

Saved workflow file = a `meta` block (name, description) plus a script body of plain JavaScript with top-level await; `agent(prompt, opts)` spawns one subagent (supports schema, label), `pipeline(list, fn)` runs one agent per list item.

`agent(prompt, { schema, label })` spawns a single subagent; the optional `schema` (JSON Schema) constrains structured output the script can then read (e.g. found.files), and `label` names the agent in the progress view.
`pipeline(list, item => agent(...))` runs one subagent per item in a list — the fan-out primitive for audit/migration/review patterns; returns an array of results.
The workflow runtime executes the script in an isolated environment separate from the conversation; intermediate results stay in script variables instead of landing in Claude's context.

Every run writes its script to a file under the session's directory in `~/.claude/projects/`; Claude gets the path at start, so you can read it, diff against a previous run, or edit it and ask Claude to relaunch from the edited version.
Constraint: no mid-run user input — only agent permission prompts can pause a run; for sign-off between stages, run each stage as its own workflow.
Constraint: the workflow script itself has no direct filesystem or shell access — agents read, write, and run commands; the script only coordinates the agents.
Constraint: up to 16 concurrent agents (fewer on limited-CPU machines) to bound local resource use, and a hard cap of 1,000 agents total per run to prevent runaway loops.

Resume after pause: completed agents return cached results, the rest run live; an agent still running when stopped isn't saved and restarts on resume — so fanning work across many small agents preserves more progress than one long agent. Resume only within the same session (exiting Claude Code restarts the workflow fresh).

A workflow spawns many agents, so one run can use meaningfully more tokens than doing the task in conversation; runs count toward plan usage and rate limits. Gauge spend by running on a small slice first; the /workflows view shows per-agent token usage and you can stop anytime without losing completed work.

Claude Code flags a run scheduling >25 agents or projected >1.5M tokens with a `Large workflow` warning (v2.1.203+); it's advisory only (doesn't pause/limit). A size guideline replaces the 25-agent threshold, and ultracode sessions suppress the warning.

Every agent uses the session's model unless the script routes a stage to a different model or the CLAUDE_CODE_SUBAGENT_MODEL env var is set (which overrides both); control cost by checking /model before a large run and asking Claude to use a smaller model for stages that don't need the strongest one.

The Dynamic workflow size setting in /config (v2.1.202+) advises Claude on target agent count: unrestricted (default), small (<5), medium (<15), large (<50); sent as advice a prompt can override, and runtime agent caps still apply.

Turn workflows off via /config toggle, `"disableWorkflows": true` in settings.json, or CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_WORKFLOWS=1 (read at startup); org-wide via managed settings. When disabled, bundled commands are unavailable and `ultracode` stops triggering and leaves the /effort menu.

Workflow loop/iterate-until-converge pattern: run a checker, fix what failed, repeat until it passes or N rounds in a row make no progress (e.g. run tsc --noEmit and keep fixing until the type check passes or two rounds show no progress).
Parallel-migration pattern: discover files, transform each one in its own isolated copy so edits don't conflict, and verify each result — avoids write contention across concurrent agents.
Audit-and-verify pattern: fan out one agent per file to find an issue, then adversarially verify each finding before reporting it (independent agents cross-check to filter false positives).
Map-reduce review pattern: run a reviewer per changed file, then hand all findings to one agent that ranks and deduplicates them into a single summary.

Choosing the right Claude model: a guide to balancing capabilities, speed, cost, and effort — with two start strategies (fast/cheap-first vs most-capable-first), a model-selection matrix, and an upgrade-decision benchmark process.
Model choice balances three key considerations — capabilities (features needed), speed (response latency), and cost (dev + production budget) — plus effort as a fourth tuning factor.
Effort parameter: recent Opus/Sonnet models trade intelligence for latency and cost WITHIN a single model; tuning effort is often a better lever than switching models.
On Claude Opus 4.8 and 4.7, the `xhigh` effort level (between `high` and `max`) is the best setting for most coding and agentic use cases.

The effort parameter defaults to `high` on Claude Opus 4.8 across all surfaces including Claude Code and the Messages API; use `xhigh` for coding, high-autonomy, and most intelligence-demanding tasks.
Tuning effort is often a better lever than switching models — a rationale favoring intra-model effort adjustment over swapping to a different model.
Fast mode (research preview) on Claude Opus 4.8 and 4.7 delivers up to 2.5x higher output speed at premium pricing; fast mode on Opus 4.7 is deprecated with removal July 24, 2026.
Option 1 — start with a fast, cost-effective model (e.g. Claude Haiku 4.5): implement, test thoroughly, evaluate, upgrade only if a specific capability gap requires it.
Option 1 is best for initial prototyping, tight-latency applications, cost-sensitive implementations, and high-volume straightforward tasks — enabling quick iteration and lower dev cost.

Option 2 — start with the most capable model (Claude Opus 4.8): implement, optimize prompts, evaluate, then increase efficiency later by lowering effort or downgrading models with greater workflow optimization.
Option 2 is best for complex reasoning, scientific/mathematical work, nuanced understanding, accuracy-over-cost applications, and advanced coding + high-autonomy agentic work.

Claude Opus 4.8 — top model for complex agentic coding and enterprise work: multihour autonomous coding agents, large-scale refactoring, complex systems engineering, advanced research, vision-heavy workflows, computer use.
Claude Sonnet 5 — frontier intelligence at scale, built for coding, agents, and enterprise workflows: code generation, data analysis, content creation, visual understanding, agentic tool use.

Claude Haiku 4.5 — near-frontier performance with lightning-fast speed and extended thinking at the most economical price: real-time apps, high-volume processing, cost-sensitive deployments, sub-agent tasks.
Claude Haiku 4.5 is explicitly recommended for sub-agent tasks — a cheap, fast model well-suited to delegated sub-agent workloads in a multi-agent orchestrator.
Claude Fable 5 (`claude-fable-5`) — Anthropic's most capable widely released model, delivering next-generation intelligence for long-running agents.
Claude Mythos 5 (`claude-mythos-5`) — available through Project Glasswing (anthropic.com/glasswing), a sibling to Fable 5.
Both Fable 5 and Mythos 5 support a 1M-token context window by default, up to 128k output tokens, and always-on adaptive thinking.
Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 are priced at $10 per million input tokens and $50 per million output tokens.
Adaptive thinking is always-on for Fable 5 and Mythos 5 — extended reasoning built into the model rather than toggled per request.
Long-running agents are a first-class use case: Opus 4.8 targets multihour autonomous coding agents and Fable 5 delivers intelligence for long-running agents.
Model-selection matrix maps need → starting model: complex agentic/enterprise → Opus 4.8; frontier-at-scale coding/agents → Sonnet 5; fast+economical strong reasoning + sub-agents → Haiku 4.5.

Upgrade/change decision process: create use-case-specific benchmark tests (the most important step), test with actual prompts/data, compare accuracy/quality/edge-case handling across models, weigh performance vs cost.
Having a good evaluation set is the most important step in deciding whether to upgrade or change models — benchmark tests specific to your use case come first.
Efficiency-optimization pattern: once implemented on a capable model, increase efficiency over time by lowering effort or downgrading models as workflow optimization improves.

Introducing Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5: launch page covering the two models' capabilities, IDs, shared 1M-context/128k-output specs and $10/$50 pricing, the Fable-5-only refusal/fallback/billing changes, availability (June 9 2026), and Messages-API behaviors (always-on adaptive thinking, no raw CoT, supported features).

Claude Fable 5 (API id `claude-fable-5`) — Anthropic's most capable widely released model, built for the most demanding reasoning and long-horizon agentic work; generally available and the recommended default for orchestrators.
Claude Mythos 5 (API id `claude-mythos-5`) — shares Fable 5's capabilities WITHOUT safety classifiers, limited-availability via Project Glasswing, successor to Claude Mythos Preview.
Shared Fable 5 / Mythos 5 specs: 1M-token context window by default, up to 128k output tokens per request.
Fable 5 / Mythos 5 pricing: $10 USD per million input tokens, $50 USD per million output tokens — the cost basis an orchestrator uses for model/effort routing.
Fable 5 includes safety classifiers that can decline requests; Mythos 5 does NOT include them — so refusal/fallback/billing handling applies to Fable 5 only.

Refusal handling: when Fable 5 declines, the Messages API returns `stop_reason: "refusal"` as a successful HTTP 200 (not an error) and reports which classifier declined — integrations must add new response handling for this.
Server-side fallback: pass the `fallbacks` parameter and the API retries a refused request on another Claude model automatically (beta on Claude API and Claude Platform on AWS).
Client-side fallback: use the SDK middleware to retry a refused request from the client on any platform.
Manual fallback: build the retry yourself on any platform/language when a request is refused.
Refusal billing: you are NOT billed for a request refused before any output is generated; on retry, fallback credit refunds the prompt-cache cost of switching so you don't pay it twice.

WHY plan for refusals: a request Fable 5 refuses can usually be served by another Claude model, so integrations should build refusal response handling + a fallback retry path + new billing rules rather than treating a refusal as a hard failure.

Availability (both live June 9 2026): Fable 5 is GA on Claude API, Amazon Bedrock, Claude Platform on AWS, Google Cloud (Vertex AI), and Microsoft Foundry; Mythos 5 is limited-availability via Project Glasswing (contact account team).
Data retention: Fable 5 and Mythos 5 carry 30-day data retention, are NOT available under zero data retention, and are designated Covered Models.
Adaptive thinking is always on for Fable 5 / Mythos 5 — the ONLY thinking mode; it applies whenever `thinking` is unset, and `thinking: {"type": "disabled"}` is NOT supported.
The effort parameter controls thinking depth (and thus cost) on Fable 5 / Mythos 5 — the lever an orchestrator uses to trade reasoning depth against token spend instead of disabling thinking.
Raw chain-of-thought is never returned on Fable 5 / Mythos 5; `thinking.display` chooses `"summarized"` (readable summary) or `"omitted"` (default; empty `thinking` field) for the thinking blocks.
Multi-turn thinking rule: pass thinking blocks back unchanged in multi-turn conversations on the same model (cross-model handling documented separately).
Task budgets (beta; set header `task-budgets-2026-03-13`) — supported at launch; caps agentic work, a cost/loop-control lever for long-horizon orchestration.
Memory tool supported at launch on Fable 5 / Mythos 5 — persistent state for agents.
Code execution tool supported at launch on Fable 5 / Mythos 5.
Programmatic tool calling supported at launch — lets the model orchestrate tool invocations in code.
Tool-result clearing via context editing (beta; header `context-management-2025-06-27`) — supported at launch; frees context in long agent loops.
Compaction supported at launch on Fable 5 / Mythos 5 — condenses conversation to extend long-horizon runs.
Vision supported at launch on Fable 5 / Mythos 5.
Migration paths: from Claude Mythos Preview → Mythos 5, and from Claude Opus 4.8 → Fable 5, per the migration guide.
Scope note: the Messages-API behaviors here (always-on adaptive thinking, hidden raw CoT) are specific to Fable 5 / Mythos 5; the Messages API is unchanged for Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku.

What's new in Claude Opus 4.8: launch summary of new model (claude-opus-4-8, 1M context, 128k output, adaptive thinking), features (mid-conversation system messages, refusal stop_details, effort=high default, fast mode, 1024-token cache minimum), inherited API constraints, and behavior changes for agentic coding
Claude Opus 4.8 has API model ID `claude-opus-4-8`, built for complex agentic coding and enterprise work, building on Claude Opus 4.7
Claude Opus 4.8 supports the 1M-token context window by default on the Claude API, Amazon Bedrock, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Foundry, plus 128k max output tokens

Mid-conversation system messages: Opus 4.8 accepts `role: "system"` messages immediately after a user turn in the messages array, letting you append updated instructions in a long-running conversation without restating the full system prompt; no beta header required
Appending mid-conversation system messages preserves prompt-cache hits on earlier turns and reduces input cost on agentic loops (vs restating the full system prompt)

Refusal `stop_details` object (since 4.7, now documented) describes the CATEGORY of refusal alongside the `refusal` stop reason, so an application can tell apart classes of declined request and route the user to the right next step; no beta header required
The effort parameter defaults to `high` on Claude Opus 4.8 across all surfaces including the Claude API and Claude Code; an explicitly set effort value is unchanged
Fast mode (research preview on Claude API for Opus 4.8): set `speed: "fast"` with beta header `fast-mode-2026-02-01` to get up to 2.5x higher output tokens/sec from the same model at premium pricing
Minimum cacheable prompt length on Opus 4.8 is 1,024 tokens, down from 2,048 on Opus 4.7; prompts too short to cache before can now create cache entries with no code changes
Setting `temperature`, `top_p`, or `top_k` to a non-default value returns a 400 error on Opus 4.8 (inherited from 4.7); omit them and use prompting to guide behavior
Adaptive thinking is the only thinking mode: Opus 4.8 does not support extended thinking budgets; `thinking: {type: "enabled", budget_tokens: N}` returns a 400 error

Adaptive thinking (`thinking: {type: "adaptive"}`) makes Opus 4.8 trigger reasoning only when the turn needs it — responds directly on simple lookups/short agentic steps, reasons on complex multistep problems; thinking is OFF unless explicitly set
Adaptive thinking reduces wasted thinking tokens on bimodal workloads vs Opus 4.7 at the same effort level, because the model decides per turn whether to think
Thinking depth is controlled by the effort parameter passed in the top-level `output_config` field (e.g. `output_config: {effort: "high"}`); the model determines when and how much to think per turn

Migration from Opus 4.6/earlier: replace `model` with claude-opus-4-8, switch `thinking` from `{type:enabled, budget_tokens:N}` to `{type:adaptive}`, and add `output_config.effort`; removing the thinking field entirely runs requests without thinking
Opus 4.8 targets long-horizon agentic coding: better long-context handling, fewer compactions, and better compaction recovery vs 4.7
Reasoning-effort calibration improved: more reliable behavior at each effort level across a range of domains vs 4.7
Better tool triggering: Opus 4.8 is less likely to skip a tool call the task required (an issue some users reported on 4.7)
Effort levels recalibrated vs 4.7: `medium` allows somewhat more thinking, `high` somewhat less, `xhigh` substantially more — re-baseline cost and latency at a tuned level before adjusting
Better compaction handling and long-context quality: long agentic traces stay on task with fewer derailments after compaction
Behavior changes in 4.8 are not API breaking changes but might require prompt updates; inherited 4.7 constraints apply to the Messages API only and Claude Managed Agents are unaffected
The Claude API skill can automatically apply the 4.8 migration steps to a Claude Code / Agent SDK codebase

Prompting Claude Fable 5 / Mythos 5: behavioral differences vs Opus 4.8 and the prompting + scaffolding patterns (effort, instruction-following, long runs, memory, subagents, verification) needed to migrate.

Best outcomes come from applying Fable 5 to the hardest unsolved, long-running, ambiguous problems; testing only on simpler workloads undersells its capability range — though it still handles straightforward tasks reliably.
A capability jump is itself a prompt to re-evaluate which instructions, tools, and guardrails are still needed — older scaffolding built for weaker models may now hurt.

Fable 5 / Mythos 5 API changes: adaptive thinking only, summarized-only thinking output, NO extended-thinking budgets, plus a `refusal` stop reason with fallback handling (details in the model intro doc).

Fable 5 runs safety classifiers targeting offensive cyber (exploits/malware/attack tooling), bio/life-sciences (lab methods, molecular mechanisms), and extraction of the model's summarized thinking; benign work in those areas can also trigger them.
Declined requests can be auto-rerouted by configuring server-side or client-side fallback to Claude Opus 4.8; domain refusals return stop_reason: "refusal".
Fable 5 is significantly more dependable at dispatching and sustaining PARALLEL subagents and reliably manages ongoing communication with long-running subagents and peer agents.
Long-horizon autonomy: Fable 5 sustains productive output over multiday, goal-directed runs with strong instruction retention across long, complex tasks.
First-shot correctness: Fable 5 produces single-pass implementations of well-specified complex systems that previously took days of iteration.
Bug-finding recall (outside safety-classified cyber domains) is noticeably higher than Opus 4.8, including search across codebases and repository history.

Longer turns by default: individual hard-task requests can run many minutes at higher effort, and autonomous runs can extend for hours — adjust client timeouts, streaming, and progress indicators before migrating.
Restructure harnesses to check on long runs ASYNCHRONOUSLY (e.g. scheduled jobs) rather than blocking a synchronous request until completion.

Anti-overplanning prompt: 'When you have enough information to act, act' — don't re-derive established facts, re-litigate settled decisions, or narrate unpursued options; give a recommendation not a survey (excludes thinking blocks).

Effort is the primary control over the intelligence/latency/cost trade-off on Fable 5: default `high`, `xhigh` for capability-sensitive work, `medium`/`low` for routine — low settings still often beat prior models' `xhigh`.

Reduce effort if a task completes but takes longer than necessary or if you want a quicker, more interactive style; at higher effort Fable 5 can over-gather context and over-deliberate on routine work.

Anti-tidying prompt for high effort: don't add features/refactors/abstractions beyond the task, no error handling for impossible scenarios, validate only at system boundaries (user input, external APIs), trust internal code and framework guarantees.

Instruction-following is strong enough to steer most behaviors with ONE brief instruction rather than enumerating each behavior by name (e.g. a short brevity instruction replaces listing every verbose pattern).

Brevity pattern: 'Lead with the outcome' — first sentence answers what happened/what you found (the TLDR), detail after; keep output short by being selective, not by compressing into fragments/abbreviations/arrow-chains/jargon.

Checkpoint pattern: pause for the user only when work genuinely requires it — a destructive/irreversible action, a real scope change, or input only they can provide — then ask and END the turn rather than ending on a promise.
On long runs, instruct Fable 5 to audit each progress claim against an actual tool result from the session before reporting; this nearly eliminated fabricated status reports in Anthropic testing.

Fable 5 can take unrequested actions (drafting an email, defensive git-branch backups); define explicit do/don't constraints — when the user is describing/asking, the deliverable is your assessment: report and stop, don't fix until asked.
Before a state-changing command (restart, delete, config edit), verify the evidence supports that specific action — a signal that pattern-matches a known failure may have a different cause.

Fable 5 dispatches parallel subagents readily: use subagents frequently, give explicit guidance on when delegation is appropriate, and prefer asynchronous orchestrator↔subagent communication over blocking until each returns.
Long-lived subagents that keep context across subtasks save time and cost via cache reads and avoid bottlenecking on the slowest subagent.
Delegation prompt: 'Delegate independent subtasks to subagents and keep working while they run; intervene if a subagent goes off track or is missing relevant context.'

Construct a memory system: give Fable 5 a place (as simple as a Markdown file) to record one lesson per file with a one-line summary, capturing corrections and confirmed approaches, updating rather than duplicating and deleting wrong notes.
Bootstrap memory from history: have Fable 5 use subagents to reflect on previous sessions, extract core themes/lessons, store them in a location [X], and know to reference [X] going forward.

Rare early stopping: deep in a session Fable 5 may end a turn with a text-only intent ('I'll now run X') without the tool call, or pause for permission it doesn't need; a 'continue' / 'go ahead end to end' suffices.

Autonomous-pipeline system reminder: user isn't watching and can't answer mid-task, so don't ask 'Want me to…?'; proceed on reversible actions; before ending the turn, if the last paragraph is a plan/question/promise, DO that work with tool calls now.

Rare context-budget concern: in very long sessions Fable 5 may suggest a new session or trim its work, usually triggered by a harness-shown remaining-token countdown — avoid surfacing explicit context counts.
Context-reassurance prompt: 'You have ample context remaining. Do not stop, summarize, or suggest a new session on account of context limits. Continue the work.'

Give the reason not only the request: Fable 5 performs better knowing intent — template 'I'm working on [larger task] for [who], they need [what output enables], with that in mind: [request]' — especially for agents spanning multiple workstreams.

Readability addendum: terse shorthand is fine between tool calls, but the final summary is for a reader who saw none of it — re-ground with outcome first, drop working shorthand/arrow-chains/invented labels, spell out identifiers.

send_to_user tool: a client-side tool whose input is a message rendered verbatim in the UI (tool inputs are never summarized) so a long async agent can surface deliverables/progress/answers WITHOUT ending its turn; returns a simple ack.

Defining send_to_user isn't enough — without a system-prompt instruction Fable 5 rarely calls it; pair it with elicitation language and use it ONLY for verbatim user-facing content, never narration or reasoning.
Scaffolding: start at the top of your difficulty range — pick a task harder than you'd give prior models and have Fable 5 scope it, ask clarifying questions, and execute.

Make self-verification explicit: separate fresh-context verifier subagents outperform self-critique — instruct the model to establish a checking method at interval [X] and verify its work with subagents against the specification.

Refactor existing prompts/skills: skills built for prior models are often too prescriptive and degrade Fable 5 output — review, consider removing older instructions, and note Fable 5 updates skills on the fly from what it learns.

Don't instruct Claude to echo/transcribe/explain its internal reasoning as response text — it can trigger the reasoning_extraction refusal category and elevated fallbacks to Opus 4.8; read structured `thinking` blocks from adaptive thinking instead.
Managed Agents 'Define outcomes': tell an agent what 'done' looks like via a rubric-graded outcome, and it self-evaluates and iterates in a loop until the outcome is met or iterations run out

An outcome tells a session the target end result and how to measure its quality; the agent works toward it, self-evaluating and iterating until met — the core loop primitive for autonomous, goal-directed sessions

When an outcome is defined the harness auto-provisions a 'grader' that evaluates the produced artifact against the rubric and returns an explanation of which criteria passed/failed, fed back to the agent for the next iteration
The grader runs in a SEPARATE context window so it is not influenced by the main agent's implementation choices — an independent verifier isolated from the worker

Managed Agents API requests need the 'managed-agents-2026-04-01' beta header (memory store endpoints use 'agent-memory-2026-07-22' instead); the SDK sets it automatically and it also grants Files API access

A rubric is a REQUIRED markdown document of per-criterion scoring; the grader scores each criterion independently, passed inline as text on user.define_outcome or uploaded via the Files API for reuse across sessions
Write rubrics as explicit gradeable criteria ('CSV contains a price column with numeric values') not vague ones ('data looks good'); vague criteria produce noisy independent-criterion evaluations

Upload a rubric via Files API (POST /v1/files, client.beta.files.upload) to get a file_id reusable across sessions; referenced as rubric {type:'file', file_id:...} vs inline {type:'text', content:...}
user.define_outcome event initiates an outcome: fields description, rubric ({type:text|file}), and optional max_iterations; echoed back on receipt with processed_at timestamp and an outcome_id
max_iterations on user.define_outcome is optional: default 3, max 20 — the cap on grader-driven revision cycles, the primary knob bounding cost/effort of an autonomous outcome loop
Sending a user.define_outcome event makes the agent begin work IMMEDIATELY on receipt — no additional user.message event is required to start
An outcome can be defined inside the session create call by passing a single user.define_outcome event in initial_events, creating the session and starting outcome work in one call

Outcome progress surfaces on the events stream: agent.* events show work-in-progress; span.outcome_evaluation_* events are emitted ONLY for outcome-oriented sessions and show iteration count and grader feedback process

You may send user.message events to an outcome-oriented session to steer the agent mid-run, but it is not required — the agent iterates toward the outcome on its own until it succeeds or runs out of iterations
A user.interrupt event pauses work on the current outcome and marks span.outcome_evaluation_end.result as 'interrupted', letting you kick off a new outcome

Only one outcome is supported at a time, but outcomes can be CHAINED in sequence by sending a new user.define_outcome after the terminal span.outcome_evaluation_end; session retains prior-outcome history and can continue conversationally
The iteration field is a 0-indexed revision counter (0 = first evaluation, 1 = re-evaluation after first revision); the grader re-evaluates after each agent revision until satisfied or capped
span.outcome_evaluation_start fires once when the grader begins evaluating one iteration loop, carrying id, outcome_id, iteration, processed_at
span.outcome_evaluation_ongoing is a heartbeat while the grader runs; the grader's internal reasoning is OPAQUE — you see it working, not what it's thinking
span.outcome_evaluation_end is emitted when an evaluation cycle ends, carrying result, explanation, iteration, and usage; result field indicates what happens next

outcome_evaluation_end.result states: satisfied→session idle; needs_revision→new iteration cycle; max_iterations_reached→one final ack turn then idle; failed→idle (rubric doesn't apply/contradicts description); interrupted→interrupted mid-outcome

Check outcome status by listening for span.outcome_evaluation_end OR polling GET /v1/sessions/{id} and reading outcome_evaluations[].result; pre-completion result reports pending, running, or evaluating

Each outcome_evaluation_end reports a usage block (input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens) — the grader's token cost per iteration, observable for cost control
The agent writes output files to /mnt/session/outputs/ inside the sandbox; once the session is idle, fetch them via the Files API scoped to the session
List session deliverables with GET /v1/files?scope_id=<session_id>; scope_id filtering requires the managed-agents-2026-04-01 beta header explicitly on the files request

Multiagent orchestration lets one coordinator agent delegate to a roster of up to 20 other agents within a single session, each running in its own context-isolated session thread with its own model/prompt/tools while sharing sandbox, filesystem, and vault credentials — enabling parallelization, specialization, and escalation patterns via the Managed Agents API.

The coordinator is the top-level agent that reports on the primary thread and spawns additional session threads at runtime when it delegates work; it can only delegate to ONE level of agents (referencing an agent that has its own multiagent.agents roster fails create/update with a validation error).

Each agent runs in its own session thread — a context-isolated event stream with its own conversation history; tools, MCP servers, and context are NOT shared between agents, though sandbox, filesystem, and vault credentials ARE shared.
Threads are persistent: the coordinator can send a follow-up to an agent it called earlier, and that agent retains everything from its previous turns.

Each agent uses its own configuration: model, system prompt, tools, MCP servers, and skills — the exception being session-level agent configuration overrides, which apply only to the coordinator and its `self` copies.

Multiagent coordination best suits complex tasks spanning many surfaces or where multiple well-scoped subtasks contribute to one goal — three working patterns: Parallelization, Specialization, Escalation.
Parallelization pattern: fan out independent subtasks simultaneously (searching multiple sources, analyzing separate files) and have the coordinator synthesize the results.
Specialization pattern: route to agents with domain-focused system prompts and tools (e.g. a security agent, a documentation agent) rather than loading one agent with every capability.
Escalation pattern: consult a more capable agent or model for a subset of complex subtasks.
`multiagent` config on an agent declares the delegation roster: set `type: coordinator` plus an `agents` array; the coordinator uses the `agent_toolset_20260401` tool to delegate to roster members.
`agent_toolset_20260401` is the tool type added to a coordinator's `tools` that gives it the ability to delegate to the agents declared in its multiagent roster.

Roster entry `{"type":"agent","id":agent.id}` references a previously created agent by ID; with no `version` it pins to the latest version at coordinator creation time, and `{...,"version":agent.version}` pins a specific version.
Roster entry `{"type":"self"}` lets the coordinator spawn copies of itself; session-level agent configuration overrides also apply to these self copies (but not to roster entries referenced by ID).

The coordinator's configuration including its multiagent.agents roster is snapshotted at create/update time; referenced agents stay pinned and do NOT auto-pick-up later definition updates — you must update the coordinator to delegate to a newer version.
A maximum of 20 unique agents can be listed in multiagent.agents, but the coordinator can call multiple copies of each agent.

The coordinator can only delegate to ONE level of agents; referencing an agent that itself has a multiagent.agents roster fails the create/update request with a validation error (no nested coordinators).
Example roster uses claude-opus-4-8 as the coordinator model (Engineering Lead) — a more capable model for orchestration — while delegating specialized work to other agents.
Example uses claude-haiku-4-5 as the model for a cheaper specialized subagent (the researcher), demonstrating cost/effort routing: capable model coordinates, cheaper model does scoped work.
Create a session referencing the coordinator agent (with an environment_id); the coordinator then delegates to the agents in its roster as needed during the session.
Managed Agents API requests require the `managed-agents-2026-04-01` beta header, except memory store endpoints which use `agent-memory-2026-07-22`; the SDK sets the correct beta header automatically.

MCP servers are agent-scoped (each agent definition declares its own servers/tools) while vault credentials are session-scoped (vault_ids passed at session creation apply to every thread) — so declaring only needed servers per agent limits that agent's access.
To authenticate MCP servers, include a vault credential for every MCP server used across all agents; the session's vault_ids supply credentials to the threads whose agents declare those servers.

MCP auth matching normalizes both the credential's mcp_server_url and the agent's mcp_servers[].url (scheme/host lowercased, default ports and trailing slashes stripped) — casing, default port, or trailing-slash differences still match, but a different path, subdomain, or non-default port does not.

The session-level event stream (`/v1/sessions/{id}/events/stream`) IS the primary thread: a condensed view of all activity across threads — you see subagent work start/end and blocking events (tool permission requests) but not full subagent activity.
Session `status` is an aggregation of all agent activity: if at least one thread is `running`, the overall session status is `running`.
A maximum of 25 concurrent threads are supported per session; the coordinator calling multiple copies of one roster agent creates multiple threads associated with that single agent.
List all threads of a session via `/v1/sessions/{id}/threads`; the list includes the primary thread, whose `parent_thread_id` is null.
Send `user.interrupt` with `session_thread_id` to stop a specific thread; omitting session_thread_id interrupts every non-archived thread including the primary.

Interrupting a child thread blocked on `requires_action` closes each pending tool call with an error result ("Tool execution was interrupted before completion. Please retry.") and re-emits session.thread_status_idle with stop_reason end_turn WITHOUT sampling the model; interrupting an already-idle thread is a no-op.

Archive a completed session thread (`POST /threads/{id}/archive`) to free a slot against the 25-thread limit; archive only succeeds if the thread is idle — a thread parked on requires_action counts as idle and archives directly, but a running thread must be interrupted first.

Message-direction events are named relative to the thread whose stream they appear on: agent.thread_message_received = a message arrived on this thread from another; agent.thread_message_sent = this thread sent one — a delegated task arrives on the child's stream as thread_message_received.

Primary-thread events surfacing multiagent activity: session.thread_created, session.thread_status_running, session.thread_status_idle (with stop_reason), session.thread_status_terminated, agent.thread_message_received (from_agent_name/content), agent.thread_message_sent (to_agent_name/content).

session.thread_status_idle means the agent on that thread is awaiting input and includes a `stop_reason` indicating why it stopped (e.g. end_turn, or requires_action with event_ids for a pending tool).

Each session thread has its own event stream at `/v1/sessions/{id}/threads/{thread_id}/stream` to inspect a specific agent's reasoning and tool calls; critical events are proxied to the primary thread but full detail requires the thread's own stream.

A thread stream accepts the same event_deltas[] parameter as the session stream to preview a subagent's text as it generates; a connection previews ONLY the thread it reads — child previews never appear on the session-level stream, so to watch a subagent live you must open its own thread stream.

When a subagent needs client input — permission for an always_ask tool, or a custom tool result — the event is cross-posted to the primary thread with session_thread_id identifying the originating thread; reply with user.tool_confirmation (tool_use_id) or user.custom_tool_result (custom_tool_use_id) and the server routes it to the correct thread automatically.

Claude Managed Agents overview: a pre-built, configurable, Anthropic-managed agent harness/infrastructure for running Claude as an autonomous agent on long-running and asynchronous tasks, built around Agent/Environment/Session/Events concepts.

Two ways to build with Claude: the Messages API (direct model prompting, best for custom agent loops and fine-grained control) vs Claude Managed Agents (pre-built configurable harness in managed infra, best for long-running/asynchronous work).

Managed Agents provides the harness+infrastructure so you don't build your own agent loop, tool execution, or runtime — a fully managed environment where Claude can read files, run commands, browse the web, and run code securely.

Rationale: use the managed harness instead of hand-rolling an agent loop/sandbox/tool-execution layer — it removes the infrastructure-building burden (minimal-infrastructure use case) for autonomous agents.
Agent concept = the model + system prompt + tools + MCP servers + skills; created once and referenced by ID across sessions.
Environment concept = configuration for WHERE sessions run: an Anthropic-managed cloud sandbox, or a self-hosted sandbox on your own infrastructure.
Session concept = a running agent instance within an environment, performing a specific task and generating outputs; launched referencing an agent + environment config.
Events concept = messages exchanged between your application and the agent — user turns, tool results, status updates; event history is persisted server-side and can be fetched in full.

Workflow: (1) create an agent (model/prompt/tools/MCP/skills), (2) create an environment (cloud or self-hosted sandbox), (3) start a session referencing both, (4) send events and stream responses, (5) steer or interrupt.
Send user messages as events; Claude autonomously runs tools and streams back results through server-sent events (SSE); event history is persisted server-side and fetchable in full.
Mid-execution control: send additional user events to steer/guide the agent while it runs, or interrupt it to change direction.

Use Managed Agents for workloads needing: long-running execution (minutes-to-hours, multiple tool calls), cloud infrastructure, self-hosted execution, minimal infrastructure, stateful sessions, and scheduled/cron execution.
Built-in tools: Bash (shell in sandbox), File operations (read/write/edit/glob/grep), Web search and fetch, and MCP servers for external tool providers.
MCP servers connect the agent to external tool providers; part of an Agent's definition and one of the four built-in tool categories.
The harness supports built-in prompt caching, compaction, and other performance optimizations for high-quality, efficient agent outputs.
Scheduled deployments: recurring agent runs on a cron schedule.
Self-hosted sandbox: run sessions on infrastructure you control for compliance or data-residency requirements, as an alternative to the Anthropic-managed cloud sandbox.
Stateful by design: sessions are long-running, resume cleanly after pauses, and store conversation history, sandbox state, and outputs server-side (persistent filesystems across interactions).
Beta access: all Managed Agents endpoints require the `managed-agents-2026-04-01` beta header (SDK sets it automatically); needs a Claude API key; enabled by default for all API accounts.
Within the beta, MCP tunnels and dreaming are in a more limited research preview and require requesting access to enable.

Constraint: because Managed Agents stores data server-side, it is NOT currently eligible for Zero Data Retention (ZDR) or HIPAA BAA coverage; you can delete sessions and uploaded files via the API at any time.

Rationale: the stateful-by-design choice (server-side history/sandbox state/outputs) is what enables clean resume and long-running sessions, and is precisely why ZDR/HIPAA eligibility is forfeited — the trade-off between persistence and data-retention guarantees.
